options.explanation: Invented surface in the wrong place. CLAUDE.md is always-on project context and does not support a `globs:` frontmatter key for conditional path scoping — that conditional-glob mechanism belongs to `.claude/rules/` files, not CLAUDE.md. The config looks plausible but does nothing, so the conventions either apply everywhere or rely on Claude guessing, which is the failure already observed.

options.label: C
options.text: Register a PreToolUse hook that fires on Edit/Write and shell-injects the migration conventions into the prompt when the target path ends in `.sql`
options.explanation: Over-engineered and fragile for this. A hook can gate tool calls, but reimplementing path-scoped convention loading as a custom shell matcher duplicates exactly what `.claude/rules/` globs do declaratively — and a naive `.sql` suffix check also catches non-migration SQL. It is far more machinery to maintain than the one-file rule built for this case.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep one canonical `migrations.md` and add an `@migrations.md` import to the root CLAUDE.md telling Claude to follow it whenever writing a migration
options.explanation: Prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee. An `@`-import pulls the doc into always-on context, but binding it to migration files still depends on Claude reading the instruction and choosing to comply on each edit — probabilistic, not the deterministic path trigger required. It centralizes the text yet leaves the same inference gap that produced local-style migrations.

questions.explanation: A is correct: a `.claude/rules/` file with a `**/migrations/*.sql` glob in its YAML frontmatter binds the shared conventions to the file pattern, so they load automatically and deterministically across all 40 scattered feature folders with no duplication. B is an invented-but-plausible config — CLAUDE.md has no `globs:` conditional-scoping key; that mechanism lives in `.claude/rules/`, so the snippet is a no-op and Claude still infers. C is over-engineering: a PreToolUse hook can technically inject text, but reimplementing path matching in a shell script (and over-broad `.sql` matching) is far more to maintain than the declarative rule built for this. D is prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee: an `@`-import centralizes the doc but relies on Claude choosing to comply per edit, the same probabilistic inference that caused the problem rather than a deterministic path trigger.

questions.text: Halfway through a security sweep, you watch the main session balloon: you asked Claude to trace every place the codebase reads an untrusted `request.headers` value across 90 services, and it has been streaming back match after match with surrounding code blocks for several minutes, eating most of the window before the trace is even complete. The hard part is still ahead of you. Once you have the full picture, a security teammate is going to join the session to talk through which sink-by-sink mitigation to standardize on, and only then will Claude patch each site to that agreed shape. You need that live design conversation and the uniform patching to happen with the right context still loaded.\n\nWhat is the most effective way to finish the sweep while keeping the patches uniform?

options.label: A
options.text: Tell Claude to keep tracing but report each match tersely, one line per site with no code blocks, so the trace fits and you stay in one continuous session.
options.explanation: Wrong. Forcing terse output trades away exactly the surrounding-code detail the teammate needs to reason about each sink during the design talk, and the truncated lines still accumulate in the main window. It is a lossy fix in the same context you are trying to protect.

options.label: B
options.text: Hand the header-tracing pass to the Explore subagent so its bulky match output lives in a separate context, then take its returned summary into the main session for the live design talk and the uniform patching.
options.explanation: Correct. Explore is built for read-only, high-volume tracing: it absorbs the verbose match stream in its own context and hands back a compact summary, leaving the main window free for the interactive teammate discussion and the consistent sink-by-sink patching, where the loaded context is what holds the patches uniform.

options.label: C
options.text: Fan the trace out to several parallel subagents split by service group, having each report its complete findings with code blocks back into the main session before you start the design talk.
options.explanation: Wrong. Parallelizing the trace is reasonable, but having each subagent return its full code-block findings into the main session pours all the verbose output back into the very window you need to preserve, defeating the isolation and refilling the context before the design talk even starts.

options.label: D
options.text: Run /clear once the trace finishes, paste in a summary you write up by hand, then bring the teammate in and patch from that pasted note.
options.explanation: Wrong. /clear discards the in-session knowledge of the irregular sinks the trace surfaced, and a hand-written note cannot recover the per-site rationale; you are manually doing, less reliably, the summarize-and-carry-forward that the Explore subagent produces automatically.

questions.explanation: B is the single defensible answer: the Explore subagent isolates the verbose header trace in its own context and returns a concise summary, preserving the main window for the live design conversation with the teammate (where interactive context matters) and the uniform patching that depends on retained detail. A keeps everything in the main context and strips the very code detail the design talk needs, a lossy in-context fix. C parallelizes sensibly but has each subagent dump its full findings back into the main session, refilling the window and defeating the isolation. D throws the trace knowledge away with /clear and substitutes a manual hand-written summary, doing less reliably what Explore does automatically.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline runs a Claude Code review on every pull request, sending only the PR's unified diff as the input. On a PR that refactors the payment-retry module, Claude flags the helper functions `backoff_jitter` and `retry_budget` as unused dead code that should be deleted, but the team lead points out both are imported and called by the scheduler module, which is unchanged and therefore absent from the diff. What change would most effectively reduce these false dead-code flags?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a firmly worded instruction telling Claude to be conservative and never recommend deleting a symbol unless it is certain the symbol is unused anywhere in the repository
options.explanation: Tempting but wrong: this asks Claude to honor a guarantee it has no way to keep. Given only the diff, Claude cannot observe the scheduler module's calls, so however the instruction is worded it still cannot tell a truly unused helper from one used by code it was never shown.

options.label: B
options.text: Upgrade the review step to a more capable Claude model, expecting stronger reasoning to infer that the helpers are probably used elsewhere
options.explanation: Wrong: the limitation is missing information, not weak reasoning. No model can deduce the existence of a call site that is not present in its context, so a more capable model fed the same diff-only input would reach the same false conclusion about the unused helpers.

options.label: C
options.text: Include the files that import the changed module, not just the diff, so Claude can see the call sites before judging whether code is unused
options.explanation: Correct: Claude can only reason about information present in its context, and the false flags happen because the scheduler's call sites are outside the diff it receives. Supplying the importing files gives Claude the references it needs to tell genuinely dead code from code used elsewhere, fixing the root cause.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-processing step that discards any deletion suggestion targeting a function defined near an import statement in the diff
options.explanation: Wrong: proximity to an import is unrelated to whether a symbol is used elsewhere, so this rule would both keep false flags and drop valid ones. It patches the output with an unreliable heuristic while leaving the actual gap, the unseen call sites, in place.

questions.explanation: The key is C. The false dead-code flags arise because the review step sends only the diff, so Claude never sees the scheduler module that imports and calls the helpers; an LLM can only reason over what is in its context, so including the importing files gives it the call sites needed to separate truly unused code from code used elsewhere, fixing the root cause. A is a prompt-when-you-need-context trap: a strong instruction cannot make Claude account for call sites it was never shown. B mistakes the problem for weak reasoning when it is missing information, which no model can infer about an absent file. D is a brittle post-processing heuristic whose proximity-to-import rule has no bearing on actual usage and would suppress valid flags while leaving the missing-context cause untouched.

questions.text: Production traces for your support agent show a recurring defect: in roughly 1 in 9 sessions the agent sends a final reply to the customer in the same turn it requests a `process_refund` call, so the refund tool never actually runs and the customer is told their money is on the way when nothing happened. Your loop runner is the piece that decides, after each Claude API call, whether to execute the requested tools and call Claude again or to hand the reply back to the customer. What should that runner inspect to make the decision correctly?

options.label: A
options.text: Whether the response contains any assistant text block; if Claude wrote a customer-facing message, treat the turn as final and deliver it.
options.explanation: Wrong. This is exactly the bug in the traces: Claude routinely emits an explanatory text block in the same response as a tool-use request. Treating the presence of text as a finish signal is what delivers the reply before `process_refund` ever executes.

options.label: B
options.text: A running counter of how many times the loop has called Claude in this session, continuing until it crosses a fixed ceiling such as eight calls and then stopping.
options.explanation: Wrong. An iteration ceiling is a runaway-loop safeguard, not the continue-or-stop signal. It does nothing for the observed defect, which is a turn ending too early while a tool call is still pending, not a loop that runs too long.

options.label: C
options.text: The natural-language content of Claude's reply, scanning for closing phrases like "your refund has been issued" to recognize that the work is done.
options.explanation: Wrong. Phrase-matching the prose is the brittle heuristic the API is built to replace, and here it actively misfires: the customer-facing sentence is generated alongside the unexecuted tool request, so the keyword appears precisely when the loop must keep going.

options.label: D
options.text: The `stop_reason` field: continue the loop on `"tool_use"`, stop on `"end_turn"`.
options.explanation: Correct. `stop_reason` is Claude's structured loop-control signal. `"tool_use"` means a tool call is pending and the runner must execute it and call Claude again; `"end_turn"` means the response is complete and may be delivered. Reading it fixes the defect regardless of any accompanying text.

questions.explanation: D is the only defensible answer: the runner must branch on the structured `stop_reason` field (`\"tool_use\"` continue, `\"end_turn\"` stop), which directly resolves the traced defect where a reply is delivered while a `process_refund` call is still pending. A is the brittle text-presence check that is literally causing the bug, since Claude emits text and a tool request in one response. C is the brittle natural-language phrase-parsing heuristic that misfires here for the same reason. B is an iteration-cap safeguard that solves an adjacent problem (runaway loops) and leaves the premature-stop symptom untouched. Distractor strategies used: brittle-heuristic-vs-documented-mechanism (A, C) and solves-an-adjacent-problem / guardrail-not-mechanism (B).

questions.text: Production logs show that in 23% of straightforward refund requests, the agent invokes `escalate_to_human` instead of calling `process_refund` to complete the refund itself. Which change should you make first to reduce these unnecessary escalations?

options.label: A
options.text: Sharpen the two tools' descriptions so each states the conditions under which it applies
options.explanation: Correct. Tool descriptions are the primary signal the model uses to choose a tool. When the agent confuses `escalate_to_human` with `process_refund`, the first fix is to make each description state its applicability so the model can tell a routine refund from a genuine escalation.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a system-prompt rule instructing the agent to prefer `process_refund` and only escalate when truly necessary
options.explanation: Incorrect. A system-prompt instruction is a probabilistic band-aid layered on top of the real signal. If the tool descriptions remain ambiguous, the model still lacks the criteria to distinguish the cases and the misfires persist.

options.label: C
options.text: Build a routing layer that classifies each request as routine or complex before any tool is exposed, then hands routine ones straight to `process_refund` and complex ones to `escalate_to_human`
options.explanation: Incorrect. A separate routing/classifier layer is over-engineering that bypasses the agent's native tool selection rather than fixing the tool definitions that drive it; the simpler documented fix is to clarify the descriptions first.

options.label: D
options.text: Merge `escalate_to_human` and `process_refund` into one resolve_request tool that decides internally
options.explanation: Incorrect. Merging the two tools hides the decision the agent should be making and removes a distinct capability, which is a heavier change than clarifying the descriptions and does not address why the agent picks the wrong path.

questions.explanation: The answer is A. Tool descriptions are the primary input the model uses to select a tool, so when it confuses `escalate_to_human` with `process_refund`, the first diagnostic and fix is to sharpen each description's stated applicability. B is a probabilistic system-prompt band-aid that fails while the descriptions stay ambiguous. C over-engineers with a classifier/routing layer that bypasses native tool selection instead of fixing the definitions. D merges two distinct tools, a heavier change that hides the decision rather than clarifying it.

questions.text: Your team shipped a `/migrate-config` skill that rewrites legacy config files to the new schema. Telemetry shows the skill works correctly when someone types `/migrate-config` by hand, but it almost never fires on its own: developers describe config-migration tasks in plain English ("convert this old settings file", "update our legacy config") and Claude just edits the files manually instead of loading the skill. The skill's frontmatter has a `name` and a body, and its `description` reads only "Helper for configs." You want Claude to pick up the skill automatically when these tasks come up. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add an `auto-invoke: true` flag to the skill's frontmatter so Claude is allowed to load it without an explicit `/` command
options.explanation: Wrong. This is the invented-but-plausible-flag trap: there is no `auto-invoke` frontmatter key. Automatic loading is already on by default and is driven by how well the task matches the skill's `description`, not by a separate enable flag, so adding this changes nothing.

options.label: B
options.text: Rewrite the `description` frontmatter to name the concrete tasks and keywords ("convert or migrate legacy config files to the new schema") so it matches how developers phrase the work
options.explanation: Correct. Claude reads each skill's `description` to decide when to apply it automatically, so a vague "Helper for configs" is the documented top cause of a skill that never self-triggers. Naming the real tasks and keywords developers use makes the match fire on their plain-English requests while the skill body stays unchanged.

options.label: C
options.text: Move the skill's body into the project `CLAUDE.md` under a heading describing config migration, so the instructions are always present in context and Claude follows them whenever a migration task appears in the conversation
options.explanation: Wrong. This is the wrong-scope/location trap. CLAUDE.md is always-on memory that loads its full contents every session; it does not give you on-demand model-selected invocation, it inflates the baseline context for every task, and it abandons the reusable skill mechanism instead of fixing why the skill fails to match.

options.label: D
options.text: Tell developers in the team README to always type `/migrate-config` explicitly instead of describing the task in words
options.explanation: Wrong. This is the manual band-aid: it relies on humans remembering the command and gives up on the automatic invocation you set out to achieve. It works around the symptom rather than fixing why Claude does not match the task to the skill.

questions.explanation: B is correct: Claude decides whether to auto-load a skill by matching the incoming task against the skill's `description`, so a vague "Helper for configs" is the documented #1 reason a skill never self-triggers; rewriting the description to name the concrete tasks and keywords developers actually use makes it fire on their plain-English requests. A is the invented-flag trap (`auto-invoke` is not a real key; auto-loading is already the default and is description-driven). C is the wrong-location trap (CLAUDE.md is always-on memory, not on-demand model-selected invocation, and it bloats baseline context). D is the manual band-aid (forcing explicit `/` commands abandons the automatic invocation the question asks for).

questions.text: Auditing last sprint's commits, your team finds that database migration files Claude Code authors are far more consistent when the prompt includes your two canonical "gold" migration scripts (naming, up/down structure, transaction wrapping). Crucially, that context helps only when you decide to author a brand-new migration—the same db/migrations/ folder also sees seed-data edits, fixes to failing migrations, and schema-diff reviews, where the gold scripts add nothing. The signal for when the context is wanted is your intent, not the file's location. You want the gold scripts loaded exactly when you choose to generate a migration, with zero overhead otherwise. Which configuration best fits?

options.label: A
options.text: Embed the two gold migration scripts plus authoring guidance in the project CLAUDE.md so they are available in every session by default.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md loads into every session regardless of task, so the gold scripts would consume context during seed-data edits, migration fixes, and schema-diff reviews—the exact work the scenario says they do not help. That is wasted overhead on every unrelated turn.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a SessionStart hook plus a subagent that inspects each request, infers whether you are starting a new migration, and auto-injects the gold scripts when it decides you are.
options.explanation: This over-engineers the problem and relies on brittle intent-detection: an inference layer cannot reliably separate "new migration" from a fix, seed edit, or diff review phrased similarly, so it will both miss real cases and inject context during the work the scenario excludes. The simpler, deterministic move is to let you signal intent explicitly.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a custom slash command (a skill under .claude/) that bundles the gold scripts and authoring rules, invoked only when you choose to generate a new migration.
options.explanation: A custom slash command backed by a skill loads the gold scripts on demand—only when you explicitly invoke it to author a migration. Intent is signaled by the invocation itself, so the context arrives exactly when it helps and adds zero overhead to seed edits, fixes, or diff reviews. This is the most efficient match for intent-scoped context.

options.label: D
options.text: Set an autoload: db-migration-gold entry in .claude/config.json so Claude Code conditionally attaches the gold scripts to migration-related prompts.
options.explanation: There is no autoload key or conditional-attachment config in .claude/config.json; this is a plausible-sounding but fabricated surface. Reusable on-demand context in Claude Code is delivered through skills/slash commands under .claude/, not an invented config field, so this option cannot work as described.

questions.explanation: The concept is matching the context-loading mechanism to intent-scoped need: an on-demand custom slash command/skill (C) is correct because it loads the gold migration scripts only when you explicitly invoke it to author a new migration, with no overhead otherwise—the invocation is the intent signal. A (always-on CLAUDE.md, taxonomy #7 wrong scope) wastes context on every unrelated session, including the seed edits, fixes, and diff reviews the scenario excludes. B (auto-detect hook + subagent, taxonomy #3 over-engineering combined with #5 brittle heuristic) replaces a simple explicit invocation with an unreliable intent-inference layer that both misfires and over-triggers. D (autoload entry in .claude/config.json, taxonomy #6 invented config) names a key and conditional-attachment behavior that do not exist—the real on-demand surface is skills/slash commands under .claude/. Only C scopes the context to the moment intent is expressed, so C is the single defensible answer.

questions.text: A lead engineer's CLAUDE.md enforces a convention — "name all new test files with the .spec.ts suffix" — reliably in her sessions. Two teammates pull the latest branch, but their Claude keeps generating *.test.ts. All three are on the same commit and the same Claude Code version, and git status is clean everywhere. When the teammates open the committed .claude/CLAUDE.md, the convention is not written there directly; instead the file pulls it in with an @-import line pointing at ~/.claude/team-conventions.md. What is the most likely cause and the right fix?

options.label: A
options.text: The rule is scoped to the lead's user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, which git never shares, so teammates never receive it. Move the convention into the project's .claude/CLAUDE.md and commit it.
options.explanation: Wrong on the diagnosis: the committed project .claude/CLAUDE.md already references the convention via its @-import line, so this is not a user-vs-project scope mistake. The rule does reach the project file; what fails is that the import points at a file the teammates don't have. The stem rules this option out directly.

options.label: B
options.text: The teammates' Claude Code never built up project context after the pull. Have each of them re-run /init so Claude regenerates the project understanding that carries the convention.
options.explanation: Wrong: /init generates or suggests CLAUDE.md content by analyzing the codebase; it does not resolve a broken @-import or fetch a file that lives only on the lead's machine. Re-running it would not surface the convention, because the missing content is in an un-shared home-directory target, not in anything /init inspects.

options.label: C
options.text: Claude Code caches CLAUDE.md after first read, so the teammates are running against a stale cached copy. Have them clear the local Claude Code cache so the convention is re-read.
options.explanation: Wrong: Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md fresh and in full at the start of every session, with no cross-session cache to go stale. Clearing a non-existent cache changes nothing, and even a fresh read would still fail to resolve an import target that is absent on the teammates' machines.

options.label: D
options.text: The @-import resolves to ~/.claude/team-conventions.md, a home-directory file present only on the lead's machine and never committed, so teammates load an empty import. Inline the rule into .claude/CLAUDE.md (or commit the target via a repo-relative path) and push.
options.explanation: Correct: the committed import line is shared, but its target lives under the lead's home directory and is not version-controlled, so only her sessions ever load the convention's text. That fits a clean git status on the same commit exactly. Inlining the rule into the committed project file, or relocating the target inside the repo with a repo-relative @-import and committing it, gives every teammate the convention.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the project .claude/CLAUDE.md is shared and committed, but it pulls the convention in via @~/.claude/team-conventions.md — a file that exists only on the lead's machine and is never version-controlled, so teammates resolve an empty import and never see the rule. Inlining the convention into the committed file (or committing the target with a repo-relative path) fixes it. A misdiagnoses this as a user-vs-project scope error, but the stem shows the project file already references the rule — the scope is right, the import target is missing. B's /init regenerates project context from the codebase and cannot resolve a home-directory import. C invents a cross-session CLAUDE.md cache that does not exist (files are loaded fresh and in full each session).

questions.text: Telemetry from your support agent shows that single-issue tickets such as "where is order #5582" close in 2-3 tool calls at a 93% resolution rate. A bundled ticket like "change my email on file, re-issue the invoice for my March order, and waive the restocking fee" averages 14 tool calls and resolves only 48% of the time. The traces show the agent tackles each ask one after another and calls fetch_account_profile again at the start of every individual ask. What is the most effective change to lift resolution on these bundled tickets?

options.label: A
options.text: Split the ticket into its asks, call fetch_account_profile once, and resolve the asks concurrently against that shared profile before composing one reply.
options.explanation: Correct. This targets both defects in the traces at once: a single fetch_account_profile call shared across asks removes the per-ask redundant lookups, while resolving the asks concurrently instead of serially collapses the inflated tool-call count, after which one synthesis step yields a unified resolution.

options.label: B
options.text: Insert a verification step that confirms each ask is fully closed before the agent may start the next one in the queue.
options.explanation: Wrong. This entrenches the serial processing that is driving the slowdown and layers verification overhead on top of it; it leaves the repeated fetch_account_profile calls untouched and would raise the tool-call count rather than lower it.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a pre-classifier that detects multi-ask tickets and tags them for priority handling and SLA tracking, leaving the agent's per-ask processing logic completely unchanged.
options.explanation: Wrong. Detecting and tagging bundled tickets changes how they are routed and tracked but not how they are worked; the serial per-ask processing and the redundant profile fetches ride along into whatever handles the tagged ticket, so resolution does not improve.

options.label: D
options.text: Route any ticket containing more than one distinct ask to a human-staffed queue where a person works each request manually, so the model only ever handles clean single-issue tickets end to end.
options.explanation: Wrong. Handing every bundled ticket to people does not scale, abandons automation for exactly the cases you want the agent to handle, and sidesteps the workflow defect instead of fixing the decompose-fetch-once-concurrent pattern the traces call for.

questions.explanation: The traces name two defects: serial per-ask processing (inflated tool calls) and a redundant fetch_account_profile before each ask. A is the only option that fixes both — decompose, fetch context once, run asks concurrently against shared context, then synthesize. B doubles down on the serial pattern with extra verification and ignores the redundant fetches. C only detects and tags bundled tickets, leaving the serial processing and redundant fetches in place. D is a non-scaling human-in-the-loop band-aid that abandons automation rather than fixing the workflow pattern.

questions.text: On a long-lived feature PR, the CI pipeline re-runs Claude Code review on every push. Telemetry shows that across 9 pushes the bot posted 47 review comments, and the author flags that roughly 40% of them re-raise concerns from earlier pushes on lines that were already refactored two commits ago. The team wants to stop the repeated comments without dropping any class of check from the review. Which change best achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Ask the PR author to manually resolve or hide each duplicate review thread in GitHub after every push so stale comments stop cluttering the conversation.
options.explanation: Wrong (manual band-aid). Hand-resolving threads after the fact never stops the bot from regenerating and re-posting the same findings on the next push; the author keeps doing cleanup every iteration. It treats the visible mess, not the generation behavior, and does not scale.

options.label: B
options.text: Give the model the earlier run's comments and have it reconcile each against the latest commits, emitting a finding only where the diff still hasn't addressed it.
options.explanation: Correct. Supplying the previous comments lets the model check, finding by finding, whether the new commits resolved each one, so addressed concerns are dropped and only live ones survive. Because it still reads the whole changeset, no class of check is removed; the repeats are eliminated at the point of generation rather than papered over later.

options.label: C
options.text: On each push, call the GitHub API to auto-minimize the prior run's comment threads so older duplicates collapse out of view in the timeline.
options.explanation: Wrong (solves an adjacent problem). Collapsing old threads only declutters the UI; the bot still regenerates and re-posts the same concerns each push, so duplication is hidden rather than removed and a genuinely unresolved finding can be buried alongside the stale ones.

options.label: D
options.text: Stand up an embedding-similarity service that drops any new comment scoring above a similarity threshold against earlier comments, regardless of whether the underlying line changed.
options.explanation: Wrong (over-engineering plus a brittle heuristic). Similarity scoring suppresses comments by wording alone with no knowledge of whether the code was fixed, so it over-suppresses legitimately new issues on already-touched lines and cannot confirm an old concern is resolved. It is heavyweight infrastructure for a job the review prompt itself can do.

questions.explanation: B is correct: handing the model the prior run's comments and having it reconcile each against the latest commits lets it drop the concerns the diff fixed while still reading the whole changeset, removing repeats without shrinking inspection. A is a manual cleanup band-aid that never stops the bot from re-posting. C only collapses old threads in the UI while the bot keeps regenerating them. D suppresses by wording regardless of whether the code changed, over-suppressing new issues and unable to tell whether an issue was truly resolved.

questions.text: Your team built a `/audit-upgrades` slash command that has Claude weigh several dependency-bump strategies (pin, range, lockfile-only) before a release. Telemetry from the last sprint shows that in 9 of 14 sessions where the command ran, Claude's later answers kept dragging in rejected upgrade plans and stale version trade-offs, derailing the actual patch work that followed. You want the command's deliberation to leave no residue in the ongoing session. Which configuration change accomplishes this?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a CLAUDE.md instruction telling Claude to summarize its upgrade deliberation and then disregard the rejected options for the rest of the session.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. An instruction to 'disregard' earlier turns cannot evict tokens already in the context window; the rejected plans remain visible and keep biasing later reasoning despite the request to ignore them.

options.label: B
options.text: Invoke the command with a `--no-persist` flag so its turns are dropped from the transcript once it finishes.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is the invented-but-plausible flag trap. There is no `--no-persist` (or equivalent) option that scrubs a slash command's turns from an active conversation after the fact; the flag does not exist on the real command surface.

options.label: C
options.text: Add `context: fork` to the command's frontmatter so it runs in an isolated sub-agent context.
options.explanation: Correct: `context: fork` executes the command in a separate sub-agent context, so its deliberation over upgrade strategies never enters the main conversation history and cannot bias the patch work that follows.

options.label: D
options.text: Run `/clear` immediately after the command so the deliberation is wiped before patch work begins.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is the manual band-aid trap. `/clear` destroys the entire session context, not just the command's deliberation, so it discards everything the developer still needs and is not a per-command isolation mechanism.

questions.explanation: The concept is isolating a slash command's execution context so its deliberation does not pollute the ongoing session. C is correct: `context: fork` runs the command in an isolated sub-agent context, keeping its reasoning out of the main conversation history. A is the prompt-for-a-guarantee trap: an instruction to ignore prior turns cannot evict tokens already in the context window. B is the invented-flag trap: no `--no-persist` option scrubs a command's turns from an active conversation. D is the manual band-aid trap: `/clear` wipes the whole session, not just the command's deliberation, so it is not a per-command isolation mechanism. The three distractors now use genuinely different wrong mechanisms (prompt-only, fabricated flag, blunt session reset) rather than the source item's split-commands / user-scoping / bash-prefix set.

questions.text: A dashboard for your billing-support agent shows that gathering the facts for one dispute now takes four API round-trips before any action is taken. Tracing a session, you see Claude emit three separate tool_use turns in a row — `fetch_account_status`, then `get_payment_history`, then `check_dispute_eligibility` — each a distinct request/response, even though all three are independent and every one is needed before the agent decides anything. The later `file_dispute` action is correctly a separate downstream turn and is not the concern. The three lookup tools work fine. What most effectively cuts the round-trips spent gathering facts?

options.label: A
options.text: Raise the executor's worker-pool concurrency limit so it can run more tool calls at the same time and clear the lookups faster.
options.explanation: More worker threads only help when several tool calls actually arrive together. Here Claude emits one tool_use per turn, so the executor never has more than one call to run at a time — there is nothing to overlap. Adding concurrency capacity addresses execution throughput, not the emission pattern that creates the extra round-trips.

options.label: B
options.text: Submit the three lookups through the Message Batches API so they are processed together as one batch job, then read all of the results back and resume the conversation once the batch returns its combined output.
options.explanation: The Message Batches API is an asynchronous, offline path optimized for cost and throughput on large non-interactive workloads, not for cutting latency in a live session. Routing an interactive dispute through batch processing adds queueing delay rather than removing round-trips, and it does not change how Claude chooses to emit tool_use blocks turn by turn.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch the agent to a faster model so each tool-use turn returns more quickly.
options.explanation: A faster model shortens the time per turn but leaves the three sequential turns intact — you still pay three round-trips. The waste is the count of trips, not the latency of each, so this trims a symptom while the structural cause remains.

options.label: D
options.text: Prompt Claude to request the three independent lookups as parallel tool_use blocks in one turn, and have the executor return all three results together before the next API call.
options.explanation: Claude can place multiple independent tool_use blocks in a single response; instructing it to do so for the three lookups and returning all results together collapses three fact-gathering round-trips into one. This uses the native parallel-tool capability with no new tools, services, or model changes, directly removing the sequential gaps that inflated the trip count.

questions.explanation: D is correct: Claude natively supports emitting several independent tool_use blocks in one response, so prompting it to issue the three independent lookups in a single turn and having the executor return all results together before the next API call collapses three fact-gathering round-trips into one — the minimal change that fixes the actual cause. A raises executor worker-pool concurrency, but with one tool_use emitted per turn there is never more than one call to run, so added capacity has nothing to overlap. B routes the lookups through the Message Batches API, an asynchronous offline path that adds queueing delay in a live session and does not alter how Claude emits tool_use blocks. C switches to a faster model, which shortens each turn but leaves all three sequential round-trips in place, trimming a symptom rather than the structural cause.

questions.text: Your four-person team uses Claude Code with a Sentry MCP server for triaging production errors, and each engineer authenticates with their own Sentry API token. A pre-commit secret scanner just blocked a push because someone hardcoded their token directly into a committed `.mcp.json`. You still want every engineer to share one identical MCP setup that lives in the repo, while keeping each person's token out of version control. What's the most effective way to configure this?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep the server in the project-scoped `.mcp.json`, but write the credential as `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` so Claude Code expands it from each engineer's environment at launch.
options.explanation: Correct. The project-scoped `.mcp.json` stays committed as the single identical configuration all four engineers share, and `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` expansion injects each person's own token from their environment at runtime so no secret is ever written to the file the scanner watches. This native pattern is the only option that satisfies both halves of the requirement at once: shared config in the repo AND tokens out of version control.

options.label: B
options.text: Add `.mcp.json` to `.gitignore` and have every engineer recreate the Sentry server locally with `claude mcp add` plus their own token.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Gitignoring the file does keep tokens out of version control, but it removes the config from the repo entirely, so there is no shared source of truth and the four hand-built setups drift apart. This satisfies the secret half while failing the 'one identical setup that lives in the repo' half.

options.label: C
options.text: Commit the `.mcp.json` but pass the token inline as `--env SENTRY_TOKEN=<value>`, filling in whichever engineer's token is current.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Writing the literal token value into a committed file is exactly the hardcoded-secret pattern the pre-commit scanner just blocked; it re-leaks a real credential on the next push and cannot even hold four engineers' distinct tokens. It fails the very constraint the scenario raised.

options.label: D
options.text: Move the Sentry server out of the repo into each engineer's user-scope config via `claude mcp add --scope user` with their personal token.
options.explanation: Incorrect. User-scope registration keeps each token private and out of the repo, but it produces four independent per-machine configurations with nothing version-controlled to keep them identical. Like B, it solves only the secret half and abandons the shared, repo-resident requirement.

questions.explanation: A is correct: a committed project-scoped `.mcp.json` using `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` expansion is the only option that delivers both halves at once -- one identical configuration that lives in the repo, with each engineer's token supplied from their environment and never committed. B (gitignore + local recreate) keeps secrets out of VCS but removes the shared config from the repo and invites drift. C (inline `--env SENTRY_TOKEN=<value>`) re-commits a literal secret, the exact hardcoded-token pattern the scanner just blocked. D (user-scope registration) keeps tokens private but leaves four un-synchronized per-machine setups with nothing version-controlled. Only A satisfies both 'shared in the repo' and 'token out of version control'.

questions.text: Production dashboards show the /reports/export endpoint's p95 latency climbed from 400ms to 9s over the past week, and the on-call log shows three timeouts last night. The endpoint streams a CSV by looping rows in app code. Three structurally different fixes are on the table: push the aggregation down into a single SQL query (fast, but the report logic now lives in the database), generate the file asynchronously and return a download link (changes the API contract and the client UX), or paginate and stream incrementally (keeps the contract but caps memory, not total time). Nobody has captured whether callers can tolerate an async link, how large the largest export is, or which query dominates the 9s. You open Claude Code. What is the best way to approach this?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct-execute the SQL push-down, since moving the loop into one query is the smallest code change and the most likely speedup.
options.explanation: Wrong: it commits to one fix on a guess. No one has confirmed the query dominates the 9s rather than serialization or network, so 'most likely speedup' assumes the root cause the dashboards haven't isolated. The defensible move is to surface the trade-offs and the unknowns first.

options.label: B
options.text: Enter plan mode to weigh the three fixes against the unknown constraints and root cause, then recommend one before writing code.
options.explanation: Correct: the requirements are ambiguous (async tolerance, export size, the dominant cost are all unmeasured) AND the three fixes diverge architecturally—one changes where logic lives, one changes the API contract, one only bounds memory. Plan mode surfaces those trade-offs and aligns on a direction before any code is committed.

options.label: C
options.text: Direct-execute the async-generation rewrite with a job queue and a signed download link so the endpoint returns instantly even for the largest possible export under any future load.
options.explanation: Wrong: this jumps to the heaviest fix and silently changes the API contract and client UX, assuming callers can handle an async link when no one has confirmed that. Adding a queue and link flow before validating the need is over-engineering ahead of evaluating the trade-offs.

options.label: D
options.text: Direct-execute a larger connection-pool and a longer request timeout to stop the nightly timeouts while the team thinks about a real fix.
options.explanation: Wrong: this treats the symptom and solves an adjacent problem—the timeouts—without touching the 9s latency that causes them. It buys quiet, not a fix, and still leaves the architectural choice unmade, so it is not the right first step in Claude Code.

questions.explanation: B is correct: requirements are ambiguous (async tolerance, largest export size, and which query dominates the 9s are all unmeasured) AND the three candidate fixes diverge architecturally rather than stylistically—SQL push-down moves logic into the database, async generation changes the API contract, incremental streaming only bounds memory. That combination is exactly when plan mode fits: explore the trade-offs and the open unknowns, then recommend before implementing. A commits to the SQL fix on an unconfirmed root cause. C jumps to the heaviest rewrite and silently changes the contract, assuming an async link is acceptable (over-engineering). D treats the symptom—the timeouts—solving an adjacent problem while leaving both the latency and the architectural decision untouched.

questions.text: On-call notices a sharp behavioral split in the support agent. Customer messages containing the word "warranty" (e.g., "is my blender still under warranty?") go straight to escalate_to_specialist about 97% of the time, never calling check_order_status first. The same question phrased without that word (e.g., "is my blender still covered?") calls check_order_status first about 94% of the time and resolves without escalating. All four MCP tool schemas and descriptions are accurate and unchanged, and the split appears even on brand-new single-turn conversations. What most likely explains the pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: From pretraining the base model associates "warranty" with escalation, and that learned prior overrides the tool descriptions whenever the word appears.
options.explanation: Wrong (symptom, not root cause). A pretraining association biases choices probabilistically and shifts with surrounding context; it would not flip almost entirely on the presence or absence of one token. The clean 97-vs-94 cliff is the signature of a hard rule, not a learned tendency.

options.label: B
options.text: The tool-selection ranker that scores each tool against the message embedding has begun over-weighting warranty-adjacent vocabulary, pushing escalate_to_specialist to the top for those phrasings.
options.explanation: Wrong (probabilistic mechanism). An embedding ranker moves scores by degrees, so warranty-adjacent wording would tilt selection gradually rather than produce a near-deterministic switch keyed to one literal word. The sharpness of the split argues against a scoring effect.

options.label: C
options.text: A literal-keyword conditional in the system prompt fires whenever the token "warranty" appears and forces escalate_to_specialist, so the outcome tracks the exact word rather than the request's meaning.
options.explanation: Correct. A hard-coded instruction that keys on the literal token short-circuits normal tool selection, which is exactly why outcomes follow the exact word (warranty → escalate) while semantically identical phrasing routes normally. Deterministic rules produce deterministic, near-binary splits.

options.label: D
options.text: The check_order_status description lacks a negative example telling the agent not to escalate covered-item questions, so warranty phrasings slip past it.
options.explanation: Wrong (misreads an accurate description). The stem states the descriptions are accurate and unchanged, so a missing negative example is not the cause; and a documentation gap would degrade selection gradually rather than cause a binary, word-triggered switch.

questions.explanation: C is correct: only a deterministic literal-token rule in the system prompt explains a near-binary split that flips entirely on the presence of one word. A and B are probabilistic mechanisms (a learned prior, an embedding ranker) that would produce a gradient, not a 97-vs-94 cliff. D blames an accurate, unchanged description and likewise could not create a sharp word-triggered switch.

questions.text: You're using Claude Code to generate a function that converts user-entered date filters (like "last 30 days" or "this quarter") into a SQL WHERE clause for your analytics pipeline. Across three iterations you've described the boundary rules in prose, but the generated SQL keeps drifting—one pass treats "this quarter" as calendar-quarter-to-date, the next as the full fiscal quarter, and inclusive-vs-exclusive end dates flip every time. Each rewrite of your instructions yields a different reading of the edge cases. What's the most effective approach for the next iteration?

options.label: A
options.text: Set the model's temperature to 0 so the generated SQL stops varying between runs.
options.explanation: This misdiagnoses the failure as sampling randomness. The output isn't drifting because of stochastic decoding—it's drifting because your prose under-specifies the boundary semantics, and Claude resolves the gaps differently each pass. Temperature 0 would only make the output consistently mis-specified; it never tells Claude which quarter or endpoint convention you actually want.

options.label: B
options.text: Ask Claude to first design a formal date-expression grammar and compile parsed expressions to SQL, so every phrase maps through one deterministic path.
options.explanation: Building a grammar-and-compiler layer is heavy machinery that still leaves the real gap unfilled: the grammar's rules must encode the same boundary semantics you have never pinned down, so the parser would just enshrine whichever interpretation Claude picks. It is premature complexity over the simpler fix of stating the intended mappings directly.

options.label: C
options.text: Give Claude the full database schema and pipeline documentation so it has more context about how the filters are consumed.
options.explanation: More context about the schema and downstream consumers addresses a different gap. The ambiguity here is in your intent—which boundary each phrase should resolve to—not in Claude's knowledge of the tables. Extra documentation won't disambiguate whether 'this quarter' is to-date or full, so the drift continues.

options.label: D
options.text: Give Claude a few concrete example pairs: each input phrase mapped to its exact expected WHERE clause, including the edge cases.
options.explanation: Concrete input-output examples directly resolve the root cause—prose being read differently each pass—by showing the exact intended SQL for representative phrases. Choosing examples that cover the contested edge cases pins down the inclusive/exclusive endpoints and the quarter convention unambiguously, replacing the failing prose channel with concrete targets Claude can match.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the failure mode is repeated misinterpretation of under-specified prose boundary rules, and concrete input-output example pairs—especially ones covering the contested edge cases—eliminate that ambiguity by showing the exact expected SQL rather than describing it. A misreads the problem as sampling randomness; temperature 0 only makes the output consistently mis-specified without conveying intent. B is over-engineering—a grammar/compiler still has to encode the very boundary semantics you never specified, so it just enshrines an arbitrary interpretation. C solves an adjacent problem—more schema/pipeline context addresses Claude's knowledge, not your unstated intent—so the drift persists. D is kept to a single sentence while B is the longest option, avoiding any length tell.

questions.text: A monorepo's root CLAUDE.md has ballooned to ~700 lines covering linting rules, commit conventions, GraphQL schema patterns, and on-call runbooks. During a review, an engineer notices Claude keeps applying stale commit conventions because nobody can find that block to update it, and edits to one section keep causing merge conflicts. The team wants to break the file into small, separately-owned files while still having Claude load every relevant guideline. Which Claude Code mechanism keeps a single CLAUDE.md authoritative while pulling in the focused files?

options.label: A
options.text: Reference each topic file from CLAUDE.md using `@`-prefixed import lines, e.g. `@docs/commits.md` and `@docs/graphql.md`
options.explanation: Correct. CLAUDE.md supports importing other files via the `@path` syntax (for example `@docs/commits.md`); Claude Code resolves these imports and loads the referenced files as part of the project memory, so the root file stays a thin, authoritative index while each guideline lives in its own small, separately-owned file.

options.label: B
options.text: Place a topic-specific CLAUDE.md inside each subdirectory so Claude loads whichever one matches the file being edited
options.explanation: Tempting but solves a different problem. Nested CLAUDE.md files scope context to where you are working in the tree; they do not let a single root file pull together commit, GraphQL, and runbook guidance that all apply repo-wide, and they supplement rather than replace the root file.

options.label: C
options.text: Add an `imports:` list to the CLAUDE.md YAML frontmatter naming the files to merge in at load time
options.explanation: Wrong because this mechanism is invented. CLAUDE.md does not use a YAML `imports:` frontmatter key to merge files; the supported in-file mechanism is the `@path` import line, not a frontmatter array.

options.label: D
options.text: List the topic files under a `memory.files` array in `.claude/settings.json` so the CLI concatenates them
options.explanation: Wrong because no such setting exists. `.claude/settings.json` configures things like permissions and hooks, not a `memory.files` array that concatenates instruction files; pointing Claude at modular memory is done from inside CLAUDE.md with `@` imports.

questions.explanation: The concept is modular organization of CLAUDE.md. The documented mechanism for keeping one authoritative file that pulls in focused topic files is the `@path` import line inside CLAUDE.md, so A is correct. B is the directory-hierarchy feature, which scopes context to a working location rather than aggregating repo-wide guidelines into one index (solves-an-adjacent-problem trap). C invents a YAML `imports:` frontmatter key, and D invents a `memory.files` array in settings.json (both invented-but-plausible-config traps). Only A names a real Claude Code surface.

questions.text: A postmortem covers a `/seed-data` skill that populates staging tables from a fixture name passed via `$ARGUMENTS`. Three findings: of 40 invocations last sprint, 9 ran with no fixture name and wrote a file literally called `seed-.sql`; in two cases the generated SQL pulled column names from an unrelated refactor discussed earlier in the same session; and once the skill ran a `TRUNCATE` against a shared table because it inherited the session's full tool access. Which single configuration change set resolves all three findings most reliably?

options.label: A
options.text: In SKILL.md, instruct Claude to refuse when `$ARGUMENTS` is empty, to disregard any schema mentioned earlier in the session, and to never run destructive SQL such as `TRUNCATE` or `DROP`.
options.explanation: All three fixes are prompt-level instructions baked into the skill template, so they depend on probabilistic model compliance. Telling Claude to 'disregard earlier schema' does not actually isolate context, and a written ban on `TRUNCATE` does not revoke the tool that ran it, so the destructive path the postmortem found stays open.

options.label: B
options.text: Add `argument-hint` frontmatter so the fixture name is prompted at invocation, set `context: fork` to isolate the skill from session history, and scope `allowed-tools` to file writes only.
options.explanation: Each finding maps to a deterministic frontmatter mechanism: `argument-hint` surfaces the required fixture name at autocomplete time, `context: fork` runs the skill in an isolated subagent context so earlier session turns cannot bleed in, and a narrow `allowed-tools` makes the `TRUNCATE` path unreachable rather than merely discouraged. It is the only option that enforces all three at the configuration layer.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch from `$ARGUMENTS` to a `$1` positional parameter to require the fixture name, pin the schema with an `@schema.sql` reference, and add a `description` field warning that the skill writes data.
options.explanation: `$1` resolves to an empty string when omitted rather than forcing an argument, an `@` reference adds the schema file but does not stop prior session turns from leaking in, and `description` only shows help text. None of the three actually restricts tool access, so the destructive finding is untouched.

options.label: D
options.text: Decompose `/seed-data` into a `/seed-build` skill that generates SQL and a `/seed-load` skill that executes it, place a validation note about the fixture name in each SKILL.md, and have a wrapper script confirm the name before either skill is dispatched.
options.explanation: Splitting the skill and adding a wrapper is an over-engineered detour that still leaves both skills sharing session history without `context: fork`, so the schema-bleed finding persists, and SKILL.md validation notes plus an external wrapper neither prompt for the argument at invocation nor scope `allowed-tools` to block the destructive call.

questions.explanation: B is correct and the only defensible answer: it pairs each finding with a deterministic frontmatter mechanism — `argument-hint` for the missing fixture name, `context: fork` for session-history isolation, and a scoped `allowed-tools` to make the destructive `TRUNCATE` path unreachable. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: all three fixes are SKILL.md instructions that rely on model compliance and never revoke the dangerous tool. C misapplies real mechanisms (`$1`, `@` refs, `description`) that look plausible but leave tool access unrestricted. D is the over-engineering/adjacent-fix trap: splitting into two skills plus a wrapper still skips `context: fork`, so context bleed and the unscoped tool access both remain. B is intentionally crisp while D is the longest option, inverting the source item's length tell.

questions.text: A trace from the research system shows that when the coordinator runs the web-search and document-analysis subagents, each subagent processes its own large pile of raw pages and long PDFs, yet the coordinator's own context stays small and holds only short summaries. A new engineer asks why the coordinator doesn't just pull all of that raw retrieved material into its own context to work on directly. What best explains why giving each subagent its own separate context window is the better design?

options.label: A
options.text: Separate context windows are required so that each subagent can be assigned a different model size, which is the only way to vary model choice per task
options.explanation: Incorrect. Per-subagent model selection is configured by the orchestration layer and does not depend on whether contexts are shared; this answers an adjacent capability rather than the reason for isolating context.

options.label: B
options.text: Without per-subagent windows the coordinator would have to spin up a separate vector database for every retrieval before any analysis could begin
options.explanation: Incorrect. A vector store is a retrieval choice unrelated to how agent context is partitioned; this invents a dependency that does not exist and over-engineers the setup.

options.label: C
options.text: Each subagent can chew through bulky raw material in its own window and return only condensed findings, so the coordinator's context is never flooded
options.explanation: Correct. Isolated context windows let each subagent absorb the heavy raw input and hand back only distilled results, which keeps the coordinator's limited context focused on summaries instead of being overwhelmed by raw pages.

options.label: D
options.text: A subagent cannot read any text at all unless it is handed a context window that is completely empty when the task starts
options.explanation: Incorrect. Subagents can operate with seeded or shared context; an empty window is not a precondition for reading input, so this misstates how context allocation works.

questions.explanation: C is correct: giving each subagent its own context window means the bulky raw retrieval (large page dumps, long PDFs) is consumed inside that subagent and only a condensed summary flows back, protecting the coordinator's limited context from being flooded with low-value raw tokens. A is wrong because per-task model selection is an orchestration setting that does not require isolated contexts. B is wrong because it invents a vector-database dependency that has nothing to do with how context is partitioned. D is wrong because subagents do not need a completely empty window to read input; context can be seeded or shared.

questions.text: Production traces show the support agent issuing oversized refunds: company policy caps any single ticket at a $200 refund, yet in roughly 8% of tickets the agent calls `process_refund` with a larger amount, and the money is gone the moment that call executes. You need a guarantee that no refund above $200 ever reaches `process_refund`. Which approach best enforces the cap?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the $200 per-ticket cap in the system prompt and instruct the agent to refuse any refund above it before calling `process_refund`.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. A system-prompt rule leaves enforcement to probabilistic LLM compliance, which is precisely what already fails ~8% of the time. A hard money cap needs a deterministic gate, not stronger instructions.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a PostToolUse hook that inspects each completed `process_refund` call and automatically issues a reversing charge whenever the refunded amount exceeded the $200 cap.
options.explanation: A PostToolUse hook only fires after the tool has run, so the over-cap refund has already been paid out before the hook sees it. Reversing the charge afterward is messy, may fail, and does not satisfy the requirement that no refund above $200 ever reaches `process_refund`.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a `check_refund_policy` tool and instruct the agent to call it with the proposed amount, then proceed with `process_refund` only when that tool returns approved.
options.explanation: This reintroduces the agent-behavior dependency you are trying to eliminate: the agent must remember to call the check first and honor its verdict. A skipped or ignored check still lets an over-cap call through, so it is not an enforced guarantee.

options.label: D
options.text: Register a PreToolUse hook on `process_refund` that reads the requested amount and denies the call before it executes whenever the amount exceeds the $200 cap.
options.explanation: A PreToolUse hook runs in code before the tool executes and can deny the call outright, so an over-cap refund is blocked deterministically before any money moves. It enforces the cap regardless of how the agent reasons, which is exactly the guarantee required.

questions.explanation: D is correct: a PreToolUse hook intercepts the `process_refund` call before it executes and can deny it, giving a deterministic, code-level gate that blocks any refund over the $200 cap before money moves—independent of the agent's reasoning that fails ~8% of the time. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: a system-prompt cap stays probabilistic, the very thing already failing. B uses a PostToolUse hook, which fires only after the refund has executed—too late, since the over-cap payout has already happened and reversing it is unreliable. C adds a `check_refund_policy` tool but still depends on the agent remembering to call it and obey the result, so a skipped check leaves the cap unenforced.

questions.text: Reviewing session transcripts, you notice Claude keeps pulling in your CI/CD pipeline configuration runbook and your test-fixture generation steps during tiny unrelated changes like adjusting a log message or splitting a function, even though no pipeline edit or fixture build is happening. Those rarely-needed runbooks currently sit in CLAUDE.md right next to your naming conventions and secret-handling rules, and you want the runbooks to surface automatically only when Claude is actually doing that work, while the conventions and security rules still apply every session. What's the most effective restructuring approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Extract each runbook into its own Skill whose description names the task, keeping the always-on naming and secret-handling rules in CLAUDE.md
options.explanation: Correct. Skills are model-invoked and loaded on demand when Claude matches the running task to a Skill's description, so the CI/CD and fixture runbooks only enter context during pipeline or fixture work. Leaving the universal naming and secret-handling rules in CLAUDE.md keeps them loaded every session, satisfying the requirement that always-on rules stay always-on while the task runbooks become conditional.

options.label: B
options.text: Turn each runbook into a slash command under .claude/commands/ that a teammate manually invokes whenever they are about to start that task
options.explanation: Incorrect. Slash commands are user-invoked: a person must type the command for the runbook to load. The requirement is for the guidance to surface automatically when Claude detects the relevant task, which is exactly what model-invoked Skills provide and a manual slash command does not.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep everything in CLAUDE.md but move the pipeline and fixture sections into @-referenced files so the content stays grouped by category
options.explanation: Incorrect. @-referenced files improve organization, but the referenced content is still pulled into every conversation. That does not stop the pipeline and fixture steps from appearing during unrelated edits, so the over-loading symptom persists.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a workflow_trigger frontmatter key to each CLAUDE.md section so the pipeline and fixture runbooks load only when that workflow is active
options.explanation: Incorrect. There is no per-section workflow_trigger frontmatter key that gates parts of CLAUDE.md; CLAUDE.md loads as a whole. The real mechanism for conditional, workflow-aware loading is a Skill matched by its description, not an invented frontmatter flag.

questions.explanation: A is the single defensible answer: Skills are model-invoked and loaded on demand by description match, so the CI/CD and test-fixture runbooks surface only during the relevant task, while the universal naming and secret-handling rules remain in the always-loaded CLAUDE.md. B fails because slash commands are manually user-invoked, not automatically triggered when Claude detects the task. C fails because @-referenced content is still loaded into every conversation, so the symptom continues. D invents a non-existent workflow_trigger frontmatter key; CLAUDE.md loads as a whole and the real conditional-loading mechanism is a description-matched Skill.

questions.text: A teammate on your Claude Code project added `~/.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md` to tweak the team's `/refactor` command with stricter type-checking, but reports that Claude keeps running the team's behavior and ignores their personal edits. The project already ships `.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md`. What should you tell them to do?

options.label: A
options.text: Re-save the personal `~/.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md` so its newer modification time wins over the project version
options.explanation: Incorrect. Precedence between same-named project and personal skills is not decided by file modification time; the project skill in `.claude/skills/` always wins regardless of which file was edited last, so re-saving changes nothing.

options.label: B
options.text: Rename the personal skill to a distinct name such as `/refactor-strict` so it is no longer shadowed
options.explanation: Correct. Project skills in `.claude/skills/` take precedence over personal `~/.claude/skills/` skills sharing the same name, so the personal `/refactor` is shadowed. Giving the personal copy a different name makes it invocable alongside the team's version without touching teammates.

options.label: C
options.text: Add `priority: user` to the frontmatter of the personal skill so it outranks the project skill
options.explanation: Incorrect. There is no `priority` frontmatter key in the skill system; this flag does not exist, so it cannot make a same-named personal skill override the project one.

options.label: D
options.text: Edit the shared project `.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md` to branch on the developer's username and apply the stricter type-checking only for them
options.explanation: Incorrect. Skill frontmatter has no username-based conditional logic, and editing the shared project skill would change behavior for every teammate, which is exactly the outcome the developer wants to avoid.

questions.explanation: The defensible answer is B: project skills shadow same-named personal skills, so the personal `/refactor` is never invoked; renaming it (e.g. `/refactor-strict`) is the only way to get personal customization without affecting teammates. A is wrong because precedence is by scope (project over personal), not file mtime. C invents a non-existent `priority` frontmatter key (taxonomy trap 6). D both relies on non-existent username conditionals and modifies the shared skill, violating the no-teammate-impact constraint (taxonomy traps 3/7).

questions.topic: Multi-Agent Orchestration: subagent scope vs. coordinator decisions
questions.text: Reviewing a research run, you notice the web search subagent's top-ranked, on-topic result for a market-sizing query is a polished microsite that, on closer inspection, is published by an industry lobbying group and reads as sponsored advocacy dressed up as neutral analysis. The page's figures are usable and could swing the report's conclusions, but its independence is in doubt. What is the most effective way for the web search subagent to handle this page?

options.label: A
options.text: Drop the page from its results on the spot, since sponsored advocacy content fails a neutrality check, and return only the sources that read as independent so downstream agents see a clean set.
options.explanation: Wrong: having the search subagent unilaterally discard the page oversteps its role and destroys information the coordinator may still want to weigh. A local neutrality heuristic reintroduces exactly the silent, unreviewed call the design tries to avoid, and the subagent lacks the cross-source context to make an authoritative trust judgment on its own.

options.label: B
options.text: Re-run its queries to surface a neutral third source on the same point, then swap that source in for the flagged page automatically, treating a fresh search as the way to settle whether the content is trustworthy.
options.explanation: Wrong: spending more retrieval budget to auto-substitute a 'tiebreaker' source treats a fresh hit as a proxy for trustworthiness, which is a brittle signal, and it silently resolves the provenance question the subagent shouldn't be deciding. It also masks the original concern instead of surfacing it, so the coordinator never learns the top result was advocacy content.

options.label: C
options.text: Return the page in its results with the others, tagged with its source and a note that it looks like sponsored advocacy rather than independent analysis, and leave the keep-or-discard call to the coordinator.
options.explanation: Correct: this respects separation of concerns. The subagent completes its retrieval task without blocking, preserves the page with explicit source attribution, and flags the provenance concern so it cannot be missed, while deferring the keep, discard, or down-weight decision to the coordinator, which holds the broader context to judge source trust across the run.

options.label: D
options.text: Pause and ask the coordinator to rule on whether the page is admissible before it issues any of its remaining queries for this topic, so it only spends budget on sources already cleared as trustworthy.
options.explanation: Wrong: halting the queue to wait on an admissibility ruling blocks the subagent's core job and stalls the pipeline for no gain, since the page is already retrieved and fully preserved. It should finish gathering and return its results with the concern flagged; the coordinator can adjudicate trust in parallel rather than gating further retrieval.

questions.explanation: The concept is separation of concerns in a multi-agent pipeline: a subagent that hits a decision beyond its scope (here, whether a source is independent enough to trust) should finish its own task, preserve the artifact, explicitly flag the issue with attribution, and defer the cross-cutting decision to the coordinator. Option C does exactly this and is the single defensible answer: retrieval is genuinely complete and the page is fully preserved, so blocking gains nothing and discarding only loses information. Option A is the overstep-and-silent-drop trap (the subagent makes the authoritative trust call itself and destroys a source). Option B is the brittle-tiebreaker trap (re-querying and auto-substituting a third source as a stand-in for a real trust judgment, while hiding the concern). Option D is the halt-and-block trap (stalling the subagent's remaining work to wait on a ruling it doesn't need to wait for). Correct: C.

questions.text: A weekly trace review shows the agent fires `process_refund` on roughly 1 in 6 borderline messages like "this charge looks wrong and I'm pretty upset about it" — cases policy says should go to `escalate_to_human`. Clear-cut refund and clear-cut escalation requests route correctly. The team has pulled the exact borderline transcripts that misrouted. Which approach most reliably cuts these misroutes?

options.label: A
options.text: Add 5-6 of the borderline messages as examples, each paired with the correct tool label so the model sees the right answer for each ambiguous case.
options.explanation: Wrong: it puts the right cases in front of the model but as bare message-to-label pairs, with no view of why that tool wins. For a discrimination the model is already getting wrong on plausible-either-way messages, naked input/output pairs leave it to re-infer the boundary it keeps missing. This is the near-miss to D — same cases, but stripped of the comparative reasoning that actually teaches the call.

options.label: B
options.text: Skip the few-shot examples and stand up a separate intent-classifier that scores each incoming message for refund-versus-escalation intent and routes it to the matching tool before the agent ever runs.
options.explanation: Wrong: a bolt-on classifier is premature complexity for a problem a few in-context examples can fix. It adds a service to build, train, and keep in sync with policy, and it relocates the same ambiguous judgment into a new model rather than teaching the boundary where the decision is already made. The exam rewards the minimal effective change, not a new routing layer.

options.label: C
options.text: Route any message that reads as emotionally charged to a higher model tier, on the theory that a stronger model will resolve the ambiguous refund-versus-escalation calls on its own.
options.explanation: Wrong: the boundary here is a policy choice, not a reasoning-horsepower problem. Nothing in "this charge looks wrong and I'm upset" intrinsically signals money-back versus a human handoff — that line is set by how this org wants borderline cases handled, so a more capable model has no way to read it off the message. Buying capability does not communicate the rule; showing the rule does.

options.label: D
options.text: Add 4-5 of the borderline messages, each spelling out why escalate_to_human fits and process_refund does not for that case.
options.explanation: Correct: the misroutes cluster on exactly these ambiguous messages, and making the comparative refund-versus-escalate reasoning explicit teaches the discrimination the model is missing. Worked examples that show the decision process beat bare input-label pairs, a separate classifier, and a bigger model for nuanced edge-case tool selection, and they cost only a handful of in-context examples.

questions.explanation: D is the single defensible answer: it uses the captured borderline transcripts and makes the comparative reasoning between `process_refund` and `escalate_to_human` explicit, which is what teaches edge-case discrimination at minimal cost. A puts the same cases in front of the model but as bare message-to-label pairs with no reasoning, so it does not teach the boundary (the near-miss to D). B stands up a separate intent-classifier — premature complexity that relocates the same ambiguous judgment instead of teaching it. C routes emotionally charged messages to a higher model tier, but the refund-versus-escalate line is a policy choice the message itself does not encode, so more capability cannot read it.

questions.text: A coding agent on your team drafts a plain-language review summary for each pull request from the diff and the CI output it pulled. Over the last 50 PRs the summaries are never wrong, yet engineers reopened 18 of them because something material was left unsaid — and it was a different gap nearly every time: one summary never mentioned a changed public API, another skipped the drop in test coverage, a third said nothing about a newly pinned third-party dependency. You need to bring the reopen rate down without putting a person back in the loop on every PR. Which approach is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Before posting, have the agent list what a thorough review of this diff should cover, then check its own summary and flag any of those points it omitted.
options.explanation: Correct. A self-evaluation step has the agent derive the coverage criteria for this specific diff and grade its own draft against them, catching whichever material point it happened to drop. Because the missing item differs every time, a self-check tailored to each diff beats any fixed list — and it needs no human on every PR.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a system-prompt rule mandating that every review summary always list the changed public APIs, the test-coverage delta, and any new third-party dependencies the diff introduces.
options.explanation: Wrong (a fixed checklist for an open-ended gap). Hard-coding these three categories only covers the gaps you have already seen; the next reopen will be a category you did not list (a config change, a migration, a perf regression). It also relies on probabilistic instruction-following for each item rather than verifying coverage.

options.label: C
