options.text: Add a second pass that scores each summary for readability and concision, rewriting any that score low, so the prose stays consistently clear and easy to skim.
options.explanation: Wrong (solves an adjacent problem). Polishing readability does nothing about omitted material: the summaries are already clear and never wrong, they are incomplete. A clarity pass leaves the missing API, coverage drop, or new dependency exactly as absent as before.

options.label: D
options.text: Route the summary through a deterministic linter that rejects it unless it contains an APIs section, a tests section, and a dependencies section before the engineer sees it.
options.explanation: Wrong (enforces form, not coverage). A linter can demand named sections but cannot tell whether each section actually captured the material change; the agent can satisfy the structure with empty or boilerplate content, and unforeseen gap types still slip through.

questions.explanation: A is correct: a per-diff self-evaluation pass derives the coverage criteria and grades the draft against them, the only option that adapts to a different missing point each time without a human in the loop. B hard-codes the gaps already seen and misses new ones. C only improves readability, which was never the problem — the summaries are clear but incomplete. D enforces section structure but not whether the content actually covers the change.

questions.text: Your support resolution agent uses rolling-window compaction: once a conversation passes 50 turns, the oldest turns are compressed into a running recap while the last several turns stay verbatim. A billing-dispute review surfaces a recurring failure: in long sessions the agent quotes a refund figure that contradicts the one it had already committed to (e.g., it confirms "$240" early, then later tells the customer "$190" and calls process_refund for the wrong amount). The committed figure was agreed around turn 8 and, by the time the error occurs, has been folded into a recap line reading "agreed on a partial refund." What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Instruct the compaction step to copy any dollar amounts, percentages, and dates word-for-word into the recap instead of paraphrasing them.
options.explanation: Wrong (Prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee). This relies on the model reliably honoring a verbatim-preservation instruction every time it compacts under context pressure, but compaction is probabilistic and stays lossy for precise figures. Where a wrong refund amount triggers a real payout, you cannot trade a deterministic guarantee for hopeful instructions.

options.label: B
options.text: Pin the committed refund amount, order ID, and any SLA promises into a structured case-record block that is injected into every prompt, separate from the compacted transcript.
options.explanation: Correct. Compaction is inherently lossy, so the durable answer is to lift the load-bearing commitments out of the transcript entirely and keep them in a structured block re-supplied on every turn. The agreed figure then survives unchanged no matter how many turns get compressed, so the agent and process_refund always see the same authoritative number.

options.label: C
options.text: Raise the compaction trigger from 50 turns to 80 turns so more of the early conversation stays verbatim before any compression happens.
options.explanation: Wrong (delay, not fix). Moving the threshold only postpones the first compaction; long disputes still cross it and the early commitment is compressed and lost exactly as before. It treats the symptom while leaving lossy compaction of critical facts fully intact.

options.label: D
options.text: Archive the full untruncated transcript externally and run a similarity search over it to recover the original amount whenever the agent appears about to state a refund figure.
options.explanation: Wrong (over-engineering / fragile trigger). Detecting the moment the agent is 'about to' quote a figure is unreliable, and retrieval does nothing when the agent confidently states a wrong amount without any cue to fire the search. It adds a service and a heuristic where a single re-injected fact block already solves the problem deterministically.

questions.explanation: B is correct: because compaction is inherently lossy for precise values, the robust fix is to extract the load-bearing commitments (refund amount, order ID, SLA promises) into a structured case-record block re-injected every turn, outside the compacted transcript, so the figure can never be paraphrased away. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap—telling the compaction step to preserve figures verbatim depends on probabilistic compliance under context pressure. C only delays the threshold and the early commitment is still compressed once a long dispute crosses it. D over-engineers with external retrieval and a fragile 'about to state a figure' trigger that misses confident proactive errors.

questions.text: Production traces show the web-search subagent following a cited URL that now returns a hard 404, so the page can never be fetched. As you design the system's failure handling, what is the most effective way for the subagent to deal with this dead source?

options.label: A
options.text: Substitute a placeholder summary for the missing page and continue the search as if it succeeded.
options.explanation: Fabricating a placeholder result corrupts the evidence base: the synthesis and report agents will treat invented content as a real source, silently degrading the accuracy and citations of the final report.

options.label: B
options.text: Escalate the 404 straight to the end user and pause until they choose a replacement source.
options.explanation: Bypassing the coordinator pushes a low-level fetch failure onto the user and stalls the workflow; the coordinator is the agent positioned to decide whether to drop the source, find an alternative, or proceed without it.

options.label: C
options.text: Return the failure, with the URL and status, to the coordinator agent to decide the next step.
options.explanation: Correct: surfacing the error with context lets the coordinator make an informed choice—skip the source, search for a replacement, or note the gap—while keeping the failure visible rather than hidden.

options.label: D
options.text: Re-request the same URL several times with exponential backoff and only report failure once every attempt has been exhausted.
options.explanation: Exponential-backoff retries are for transient faults like timeouts or 503s; a 404 is a permanent condition that will not change, so repeated attempts only waste time and tokens without recovering the page.

questions.explanation: C is correct: on a subagent failure the documented pattern is to return the error with context to the coordinator so it can decide how to proceed, keeping the failure visible. A is the silent-swallow trap dressed as a placeholder—it injects fabricated content into the report. B surfaces the error but to the wrong party, bypassing the coordinator that should own the decision. D is blind-retry, valid only for transient failures; a 404 is permanent, so retrying is wasteful and never recovers the page.

questions.text: Post-deployment review of a scheduling assistant finds that 14% of cancellations hit the wrong meeting. When a user says "cancel my sync with the design team," the agent calls `find_events`, which returns several recurring meetings whose titles all contain "design," and the agent cancels whichever one starts soonest before calling `delete_event`. What is the most effective way to fix this?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the agent re-query `find_events` with extra filters such as attendee list and time window to automatically narrow the set down to the meeting the user most likely meant.
options.explanation: Adding filters and re-querying still produces a best guess: the tighter search can return several events or none, and it never confirms which one the user actually wants. It refines the heuristic rather than getting the one fact that resolves the ambiguity, so wrong cancellations continue.

options.label: B
options.text: Change `find_events` so it ranks the matching meetings internally and returns only the single event it judges most likely to be the intended one.
options.explanation: Returning only the top-ranked event hides the ambiguity from the agent, so it can no longer see that several meetings qualify and cannot ask which one. Every time the ranking is wrong the agent deletes the incorrect meeting with no signal that a choice was even made.

options.label: C
options.text: Train an intent-classification service that predicts the target meeting from the phrasing of the request and route its prediction straight to `delete_event`.
options.explanation: A separate classifier is heavy machinery that still outputs a prediction, not a confirmed selection, and it mis-fires whenever the phrasing is generic like "my design sync." It adds an ML component and training pipeline without ever obtaining the user's definitive choice.

options.label: D
options.text: When `find_events` returns more than one match, have the agent list the candidate meetings and ask the user which to cancel before calling `delete_event`.
options.explanation: Only the user knows which meeting they meant, so listing the candidates and confirming the choice removes the guesswork behind the 14% error rate. One extra turn before an irreversible deletion is a cheap, reliable price for cancelling the right event.

questions.explanation: This mirrors the ambiguous-result concept of source 751: when a tool returns several valid matches, the reliable fix is to surface them and ask the user before an irreversible action, not to keep guessing. D is correct because only the user knows which meeting is meant, so confirming the candidates eliminates the error. A (re-query with more filters) is a refined heuristic that still guesses and can return many or zero events. B (rank internally, return one) hides the ambiguity so the agent can never disambiguate and fails silently when the ranking is wrong. C (intent-classification service) is over-engineering that outputs a prediction rather than the user's definitive choice.

questions.text: Run traces show the document-analysis subagent calling the web-search subagent directly to grab its raw findings, then stitching both into a half-formed report that skips the synthesis step entirely. Citations come out duplicated and contradictory. Which change best fixes the architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Have each subagent return its findings to the coordinator, which then routes them to the synthesis agent.
options.explanation: Correct. In the orchestrator-workers pattern the coordinator owns the workflow: it collects every subagent's output and hands it to the right specialized component next. Routing both gathering agents' results through the coordinator to the synthesis agent restores centralized control and ensures the synthesis stage actually runs before reporting.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the direct subagent call but add a deduplication pass inside the document-analysis agent so the duplicated citations are removed before it builds the report.
options.explanation: Wrong. This treats a symptom (duplicate citations) while preserving the real defect: worker-to-worker communication that bypasses the coordinator and skips synthesis. Deduplication does not restore centralized control, and the report is still assembled by the wrong agent at the wrong stage.

options.label: C
options.text: Let the document-analysis agent send its combined findings straight to the report-generation agent so a finished report is produced without waiting on the coordinator.
options.explanation: Wrong. This entrenches the peer-to-peer shortcut and still omits the synthesis agent, which is the component responsible for reconciling contradictory and duplicated findings. Bypassing the coordinator removes its ability to sequence and oversee the pipeline.

options.label: D
options.text: Configure the coordinator to forward the gathering agents' raw outputs directly to the report-generation agent.
options.explanation: Wrong. Although this keeps the coordinator in control, it routes to the wrong next stage: report generation runs before synthesis, so the contradictory and duplicated findings are never reconciled. The synthesis agent must process the results first.

questions.explanation: A is correct: the orchestrator-workers pattern requires the coordinator to collect each subagent's results and route them to the next specialized component (the synthesis agent), which restores centralized control and ensures synthesis runs before reporting. B fixes only the duplicate-citation symptom while leaving the peer-to-peer call and skipped synthesis intact. C entrenches the bypass and still omits synthesis. D keeps the coordinator in control but routes to the wrong stage, sending unreconciled findings straight to report generation. Only A addresses both the broken control flow and the missing synthesis stage.

questions.topic: Error Propagation: graceful degradation with transparency
questions.text: A telemetry dashboard for the multi-agent research system shows that on a run about "regional EV battery supply chains," the web search subagent returned 14 of 20 expected citations (6 paywalled trade journals returned 403s), while the document analysis subagent fully parsed every uploaded PDF. The report generator subagent now has to assemble the final cited report from this uneven evidence base. Which approach to the coverage gap should the report generator subagent take?

options.label: A
options.text: Stand up a source-reliability scoring service that weights the 14 citations and infers confidence for the missing six before any prose is written.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is premature complexity. A dedicated scoring service is heavyweight infrastructure for a gap that an inline coverage note already conveys, and it cannot manufacture real evidence for journals that were never retrieved. The minimal effective move is to annotate the gap, not build a confidence-inference layer around it.

options.label: B
options.text: Compose the report from the 14 available citations and tag each section with a source-confidence note flagging the areas left thin by the six inaccessible journals.
options.explanation: Correct: this is graceful degradation with transparency. It preserves the value of the gathered evidence while propagating the coverage gaps as visible annotations, so readers and the coordinator can judge which conclusions are well-supported and which rest on partial sourcing.

options.label: C
options.text: Generate the report from the 14 retrieved citations and omit any mention of the inaccessible journals so the output reads as a clean, complete deliverable.
options.explanation: Wrong: silently swallowing the gap hides material information. A report that looks fully sourced but quietly lacks six trade journals is misleading and erodes trust in every cited claim, since readers cannot tell solid findings from under-sourced ones.

options.label: D
options.text: Re-rank the 14 citations by quality and drop the weakest ones, so only the strongest sources appear and the report reads as uniformly authoritative.
options.explanation: Wrong: this solves the adjacent problem of citation quality, not the coverage-gap transparency the situation demands. Pruning sources narrows the evidence base further and still leaves the six-journal gap invisible, so the report remains misleadingly complete.

questions.explanation: B is correct: it applies graceful degradation with transparency, shipping the report from the 14 usable citations while annotating the gaps left by the six paywalled journals so confidence is propagated downstream. A is the over-engineering trap (a heavyweight scoring service where an inline coverage note suffices). C is the silent-swallow trap (hiding the gap yields a misleadingly clean report). D is the solves-an-adjacent-problem trap (re-ranking and pruning addresses citation quality, not the coverage-gap transparency asked about, and still hides the six-journal gap).

questions.text: A trace review of your parallel research system shows that on the brief "vendor landscape for edge-inference chips," the market-intel agent and the patents agent independently chose to investigate the same three subtopics and returned heavily overlapping analysis. Across the last 40 runs, mean tokens per brief climbed from 38k to 71k while the count of unique claims reaching synthesis barely moved. What is the most effective way to address this?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the subagent pool and give each agent a tighter per-call retry budget, so the cost of any single duplicated lookup stays small even when several agents converge on the same subtopic.
options.explanation: Wrong (over-engineering that misses the cause). Adding agents and capping retries lowers the unit cost of a duplicated lookup but does nothing to stop two agents from researching the same subtopic; the redundant work — and the tokens — keep growing with the pool.

options.label: B
options.text: Insert a cheap first-pass model that screens candidate sources and forwards only the ones it judges novel to the expensive research agents.
options.explanation: Wrong (fixes an adjacent layer). A source-novelty filter dedups retrieval, but the agents still independently decide to cover the same subtopics, so they pull different-yet-overlapping sources and produce overlapping analysis anyway. It governs which documents are fetched, not who is responsible for which part of the question.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the lead agent partition the brief into non-overlapping subtopic assignments before dispatch, so no two subagents are ever pointed at the same slice of the question.
options.explanation: Correct. The overlap originates at the assignment level — both agents picked the same subtopics — so dividing the space up front removes it at the source: each subagent owns a distinct slice and parallel work adds breadth instead of repeating it. This is the coordinator's job in an orchestrator-worker design.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep both agents running in full and add a step that deduplicates their overlapping findings before synthesis, treating the doubled parallel cost as the price of coverage.
options.explanation: Wrong (treats the symptom and accepts the cost). Deduplicating after both agents finish discards redundant output only once the tokens to produce it are already spent, and the option explicitly accepts that doubled parallel cost, so it cannot move the token metric the trace flags.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the duplication starts when both agents independently choose the same subtopics, so the fix is to partition the brief into disjoint assignments before delegation. A only makes duplicated work cheaper without removing it; B dedups at the retrieval layer while the agents still pursue identical subtopics; D cleans up overlapping output only after the doubled token cost is already paid.

questions.text: The document-analysis subagent processes a batch of three sources for a regulatory-compliance report and returns mixed results to the coordinator: a 40-page filing parsed cleanly and yielded 6 relevant clauses, a vendor whitepaper parsed cleanly but contained "no sections matching the query," and a scanned PDF returned "PDF render failed: corrupt xref table." How should the subagent structure what it propagates so the coordinator can make the best recovery decisions?

options.label: A
options.text: Roll the three outcomes into a single "2 of 3 sources analyzed" completeness figure, keeping the per-source detail in logs the coordinator can request later.
options.explanation: Wrong. Collapsing the outcomes into one '2 of 3' figure (taxonomy: aggregation destroys actionable detail) hides whether the missing source failed to render or simply held no relevant content. The coordinator cannot tell whether to retry, swap the source, or accept the gap without re-fetching the logs, defeating the point of propagating the result at all.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the subagent silently retry any source that did not yield clauses, surfacing an outcome to the coordinator only once a source has persistently failed across attempts.
options.explanation: Wrong. Silent internal retries (taxonomy: silent-swallow vs. surface-to-orchestrator) hide context the coordinator needs to manage its time and token budget, and they would waste retries on the whitepaper, which succeeded and legitimately had no matching sections. Anthropic's guidance is to make failures visible to the controlling agent, not to absorb them in the subagent.

options.label: C
options.text: Flag both the whitepaper's empty match and the scanned PDF's render error as analysis failures, so the coordinator can schedule one uniform retry pass over every source that produced no clauses.
options.explanation: Wrong. Treating the empty match and the render error identically conflates a successful query that found nothing with a query that never completed. The whitepaper was fully analyzed and 'no matching sections' is itself an informative finding; retrying it burns resources and never changes the outcome, while only the render error is genuinely retryable.

options.label: D
options.text: Return each source tagged by outcome kind, reporting the scanned PDF's render error separately from the whitepaper's empty match so the coordinator can act on each one differently.
options.explanation: Correct. A render failure means the content is still unknown and warrants a retry or alternate fetch, whereas 'no matching sections' is a completed analysis whose empty result is a real finding. Tagging each source by outcome kind lets the coordinator retry only the corrupt PDF and accept the whitepaper's empty result, which is the recovery information the source question's distinction is built to preserve.

questions.explanation: D is the single defensible answer: it preserves the semantic distinction between an access failure (the render error, whose content is still unknown and is therefore retryable) and a valid empty result (the whitepaper analysis completed and nothing matched), which is exactly the information the coordinator needs to recover well. A fails by aggregating outcomes into a "2 of 3" figure that erases whether the gap is a failure or an empty finding. B fails by retrying inside the subagent, hiding context from the coordinator while wastefully retrying the already-successful whitepaper. C fails by labeling the valid empty match as a failure and triggering pointless retries on a source that was fully analyzed. Only D separates the retryable failure from the complete-but-empty result.

questions.text: During a regression review of the multi-agent research system, the trace dashboard shows that 38% of requests phrased like "compile the findings into the final write-up" are delegated to the synthesis agent instead of the report-generation agent. The synthesis agent exposes a `compile_summary` tool described as "compiles findings into a structured summary," and the report-generation agent exposes a `compile_report` tool described as "compiles findings into a finished report." What is the most effective way to stop the misrouting?

options.label: A
options.text: Rename `compile_summary` to `merge_subagent_findings` and describe it as "consolidates raw subagent outputs into an internal working draft for downstream agents."
options.explanation: This is correct because it removes the semantic collision at its source. Both tools currently start with "compile ... findings," so the coordinator cannot tell them apart from the names or descriptions. Renaming the synthesis tool and re-describing it around its true role (internal consolidation for downstream use) makes the two tools unambiguous, so requests for the final write-up no longer match the synthesis tool.

options.label: B
options.text: Insert an intent-classification step ahead of the coordinator that inspects each request, labels it as either "intermediate synthesis" or "final report," and forwards only the matching label so the coordinator delegates to a single eligible agent.
options.explanation: This over-engineers a problem that a description edit solves. The misrouting comes from two near-identical tool definitions, not from a missing classification layer. Adding a separate classifier introduces a new component to build, test, and maintain while leaving the ambiguous tool names in place for any future caller to trip over.

options.label: C
options.text: Lower the report-generation agent's selection threshold so the coordinator favors it whenever a request contains the word "final" or "write-up."
options.explanation: This is a brittle keyword heuristic, not a fix for the underlying ambiguity. It only catches the exact phrasings you anticipated; paraphrases like "produce the deliverable" will still collide on the shared "compile findings" wording, and the synthesis tool remains just as confusable as before.

options.label: D
options.text: Append example use cases to the report-generation tool's description, such as "Use for the client-facing final report," while leaving the synthesis tool's name and description unchanged.
options.explanation: This fixes only one half of the overlap. The synthesis agent's `compile_summary` still reads as "compiles findings," so it continues to look like a plausible match for "compile the findings into the final write-up." A one-sided edit narrows but does not eliminate the collision.

questions.explanation: A is the single defensible answer: it eliminates the root cause, the semantic overlap between two tools that both begin "compile ... findings," by renaming and re-describing the synthesis tool around its actual internal-consolidation role so the coordinator can distinguish them. B over-engineers with an unnecessary intent classifier when a description edit suffices. C is a brittle keyword/threshold heuristic that only matches anticipated phrasings and leaves the tools confusable. D is a one-sided fix that disambiguates only the report tool while the synthesis tool's "compiles findings" description still collides.

questions.text: A data-query subagent runs read-only SQL against the analytics warehouse to gather figures for reports. Run metrics show its failures split two ways: about 22% are transient (deadlock-victim aborts and connection-pool timeouts that clear on a re-run within seconds), and the remainder are permanent (queries that reference a dropped column, hit a missing-table error, or exceed the row-scan quota). At present any failure of either kind is raised straight to the coordinator, which then halts the report run and decides whether to re-issue the query, drop the metric, or abort. The platform team notes the coordinator is now burning most of its turns adjudicating routine re-run decisions. Which architectural change best addresses this?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure the subagent to return whatever rows it managed to retrieve with a success status, recording any failed queries only as a note in the result metadata that the synthesis step can choose to read.
options.explanation: This is the silent-swallow trap: reporting a partial pull as success means the coordinator never learns that a dropped-column or quota query was abandoned. The metric silently goes missing from the report, and the coordinator loses its ability to act on the genuinely unrecoverable failures.

options.label: B
options.text: Let the subagent re-run the transient deadlock and pool-timeout failures itself, and escalate only the permanent failures to the coordinator together with the failing query, the error class, and the metrics it did collect.
options.explanation: Correct. Errors belong at the lowest level able to resolve them: the subagent owns the routine transient re-runs, which removes them from the coordinator's queue, and surfaces only the truly unresolvable failures upward with enough context—query, error class, partial results—for the coordinator to decide knowingly.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the subagent retry every failed query a fixed five times with backoff before giving up, and on the final failure return a generic 'query failed' status to the coordinator without distinguishing the cause.
options.explanation: Blind uniform retry wastes attempts re-running permanent failures (a dropped column never resolves on retry) and adds latency, while the cause-less final status strips the coordinator of the context it needs. It neither cleanly offloads the transient cases nor escalates the permanent ones informatively.

options.label: D
options.text: Route every query failure onto a shared on-call review queue where a human operator inspects the error, decides retry-or-drop, and signals the resolution back so the subagent can resume—keeping the coordinator out of the loop entirely.
options.explanation: This is a manual band-aid that does not scale: a person adjudicating routine deadlock re-runs is slower and costlier than the subagent resolving them in-process, and it just relocates the bottleneck off the coordinator onto an operator rather than fixing where errors are handled.

questions.explanation: B is correct: it handles transient failures (deadlock aborts, pool timeouts) locally in the subagent to relieve the coordinator of routine re-run adjudication, while escalating only permanent failures upward with full context (query, error class, partial results) for an informed decision. A is the silent-swallow trap—marking a partial pull as success hides the dropped metric from the coordinator. C is blind uniform retry—it wastes attempts on permanent failures and returns a cause-less status that gives the coordinator nothing to act on. D is a manual human-in-the-loop band-aid that does not scale and merely moves the bottleneck from the coordinator to an operator instead of resolving errors at the level that has the context.

questions.text: To let the report generation agent save finished reports, you gave it a broad `write_file` tool that accepts any path. A trace audit of last week's runs shows the agent using `write_file` to overwrite cached source documents in the shared workspace and to scribble scratch notes into the web search agent's input folder, corrupting downstream runs. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a strongly worded rule to the report agent's system prompt that it must only write to the `/reports` directory and never touch shared or other agents' folders.
options.explanation: Wrong. This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: a system-prompt rule relies on the model choosing to comply, but `write_file` still technically accepts any path, so a single misfire re-corrupts the shared workspace. Soft guidance cannot enforce a boundary the tool itself leaves open.

options.label: B
options.text: Remove `write_file` from the report agent and have it return report text to the coordinator, which performs all disk writes on the agent's behalf.
options.explanation: Wrong. This over-corrects: it strips a capability the report agent legitimately needs and funnels every write through the coordinator, adding a serialization bottleneck and latency for the common case of simply saving a finished report. The agent should keep write access, just narrowed.

options.label: C
options.text: Replace `write_file` with a `save_report` tool whose path is fixed to the report output directory and only takes the report content and filename.
options.explanation: Correct. This constrains capability at the tool interface: a `save_report` tool that can only write inside the report directory makes writing to source caches or another agent's folder structurally impossible, not merely discouraged. It applies least privilege so the misbehavior can't recur regardless of model decisions.

options.label: D
options.text: Wrap `write_file` in a guard that rejects paths matching the shared cache and the web search agent's folder, while permitting all other paths.
options.explanation: Wrong. This is a blocklist that addresses symptoms: it patches the two paths seen in the audit but leaves every other sensitive location writable, so the next out-of-scope write slips through and the list needs perpetual maintenance. An allowlisted, purpose-built tool is the durable fix.

questions.explanation: The concept is tool scoping by least privilege: an overly permissive general tool (`write_file` accepting any path) lets the report agent act outside its job, and the durable fix is to constrain capability at the tool interface, not to govern behavior with prompts or filters. C is correct: a fixed-path `save_report` tool makes out-of-scope writes structurally impossible. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee distractor (relies on model compliance against a tool that still permits the action). B over-corrects by removing a needed capability and routing everything through the coordinator. D is a blocklist that only patches the observed paths and needs endless maintenance, addressing symptoms rather than the permissive-tool root cause.

questions.text: Your on-call incident-triage agent ingests one aggregated 90K-token event timeline (the opening pager alerts, then the chronological remediation actions operators ran, then the final all-clear and resolution notes) and drafts the postmortem. Across the last month of incidents, the drafts consistently capture the initial alerts and the closing resolution, but in about 1 in 4 reviews an engineer flags that a mitigation step taken partway through the timeline -- present verbatim in the input -- never appears in the postmortem. Which restructuring of the aggregated timeline most directly stops these mid-timeline steps from being dropped?

options.label: A
options.text: Append a directive to the triage agent's system prompt telling it to treat every mitigation step as equally important and never omit actions from the middle of the timeline.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This layers a probabilistic instruction on top of an attention-distribution problem; it does not change where the mid-timeline steps physically sit, so the model's positional recall bias toward the opening and closing of the timeline persists. A prompt directive cannot guarantee the model attends to material it is structurally inclined to under-weight.

options.label: B
options.text: Stand up an embedding-based retrieval layer that scores every timeline event against the incident summary, then re-injects only the top-ranked mid-timeline actions into a second drafting pass.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Building an embed-and-rerank service is premature complexity for something solved by reordering the existing timeline; it adds a pipeline and a second pass with new failure modes while the simpler fix of moving critical steps into well-attended positions is available. The dropped steps are present in the input, so they are mis-positioned, not unavailable.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the on-call engineer diff each draft against the raw timeline and manually paste back any mitigation step the agent skipped before the postmortem is published.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A manual reconciliation step is a human-in-the-loop band-aid that does not scale and leaves the underlying positional drop in place; it papers over the symptom on every incident rather than fixing why the agent loses mid-timeline content. The exam rewards the architectural fix, not recurring manual cleanup.

options.label: D
options.text: Lead the timeline with a summary of the mitigation steps taken and close it with a recap of those same steps, bookending the buried actions into the primacy and recency positions.
options.explanation: Correct. The drops track a positional pattern: events at the start (primacy) and end (recency) are recalled reliably while the middle is lost. Surfacing the mid-timeline mitigation steps into both the leading and trailing positions places them where the model attends most reliably, directly counteracting the lost-in-the-middle effect that causes the omissions.

questions.explanation: The traces describe the classic lost-in-the-middle position effect: content at the beginning and end of a long context is recalled reliably while middle content is dropped. D fixes this at its root by relocating the mid-timeline mitigation steps into the high-attention primacy and recency positions. A is a system-prompt directive that cannot override the model's structural positional bias. B over-engineers a retrieval-and-rerank service when a simple reorder suffices, since the steps are present in the input, just badly placed. C is a manual human-in-the-loop band-aid that does not scale and never addresses why the agent loses the middle. Correct answer: D.

questions.text: In your CI pipeline, one Claude Code step generates integration tests for each new endpoint and, in the same run, judges whether those tests adequately cover the endpoint before merge. Over the last sprint, 9 endpoints shipped with tests Claude rated "comprehensive," yet four later failed in staging on input combinations Claude's own generation notes had flagged as "edge cases worth covering." The judging output reuses the generation transcript. Which change most directly removes the root cause of these missed gaps?

options.label: A
options.text: Run the coverage judgment as a separate Claude instance that sees only the endpoint and the generated tests, not the generation transcript.
options.explanation: Correct. The root cause is self-assessment bias: the same instance that wrote the tests and rationalized which edge cases to skip is then grading its own work with full access to that reasoning, so it confirms its earlier conclusions. A fresh instance given only the endpoint and the tests evaluates coverage independently, the way a second reviewer would, and can flag the gaps the generator talked itself out of.

options.label: B
options.text: Append a self-critique instruction to the generation prompt so Claude lists uncovered edge cases and rates coverage before emitting the tests.
options.explanation: Tempting but wrong (prompt-when-you-need-independence). This keeps judgment inside the same context that already flagged those edge cases as 'worth covering' and then dropped them; the same bias that rationalized skipping them will rationalize the self-critique, so the gaps persist.

options.label: C
options.text: Turn on extended thinking for the generation step so Claude deliberates longer over which edge cases the tests must exercise.
options.explanation: Wrong (adjacent problem). The transcript shows Claude already identified the edge cases and still rated the tests comprehensive, so the failure is not insufficient deliberation during generation; more thinking time does not remove the self-review blind spot at the judging stage.

options.label: D
options.text: Feed the endpoint's full request/response schema and prior incident reports into the generation context so coverage decisions are better informed.
options.explanation: Wrong (adjacent problem / more context). Richer input may improve generation, but the notes prove the relevant edge cases were already known and consciously deprioritized. The defect is one instance grading itself, not a shortage of information, so added context does not address it.

questions.explanation: A is correct: a separate Claude instance that judges coverage without the generation transcript breaks the self-assessment confirmation bias, which is the root cause of gaps the generator already flagged then rationalized away. B fails because self-critique stays in the same biased context. C fails because the edge cases were already identified, so more deliberation does not fix a judging-stage blind spot. D fails because the information was already present and the cases were knowingly skipped, so more context is not the fix.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline has Claude triage each pull-request comment into one of three labels—"blocker", "warning", or "nit". Auditing last week's runs, you find the same kind of finding (an unhandled promise rejection) tagged "blocker" on one PR and "nit" on another, so teams no longer know which comments actually gate a merge. What is the most effective way to make the label assignments consistent across PRs?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a line to the prompt instructing Claude to apply the three labels firmly and consistently across every pull request, emphasizing how important predictable labeling is.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Telling the model to 'be consistent' is exhortation, not a definition. Without stated criteria for what separates a blocker from a warning from a nit, each run still interprets the boundaries on its own, so the same finding keeps drifting between labels. Emphasis alone gives the model no fixed reference point to anchor against.

options.label: B
options.text: Define explicit, absolute criteria for blocker, warning, and nit in the prompt, each illustrated with concrete example findings.
options.explanation: Correct. The inconsistency comes from the model interpreting the three labels differently each run. Stating absolute criteria for each label and anchoring them with concrete example findings gives the model stable reference points, so equivalent findings land on the same label across PRs—directly addressing the root cause.

options.label: C
options.text: Set the model's temperature to 0 for the triage call so the classification becomes deterministic and stops varying between runs.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Temperature 0 only makes a single call with identical input reproducible; it solves run-to-run randomness, not the problem here. Each PR is a different input, and without a shared definition of the labels the model still draws the blocker/warning/nit boundaries differently from one PR to the next, so the same finding keeps landing on different labels.

options.label: D
options.text: Fine-tune a dedicated classifier on past labeled comments and route every triage decision to that model instead of prompting Claude.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Training a separate classifier is heavy machinery for a problem whose root cause is an underspecified prompt. It needs a labeled corpus and ongoing retraining, and if the label definitions themselves are ambiguous it will simply learn the same inconsistency from the noisy training data. The minimal effective fix is to specify the criteria, not build a new model.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the labels drift because the model lacks a stable definition of each tier, and explicit absolute criteria anchored by concrete example findings give it fixed reference points so equivalent findings get the same label across PRs. A (firmer instruction to 'be consistent') is exhortation without criteria, so the model still sets the boundaries differently each run. C (temperature 0) only removes run-to-run randomness on identical input; each PR is a different input, so cross-PR boundary drift persists without shared definitions. D (fine-tuned classifier) is over-engineering—it needs a labeled corpus and retraining and will reproduce the same ambiguity, when the minimal effective change is to specify the criteria in the prompt.

questions.text: Your CI job runs the Claude Code CLI in `--print` mode to generate unit tests for changed files, and the suggestions are solid. But the pipeline's pass/fail gate consumes JUnit XML, and right now Claude returns a prose report ("Test A covers the happy path and passes; Test B for the empty-input case fails..."). A flaky hand-written extractor mis-reads about 1 in 8 runs, so the gate sometimes goes green on a build that should be red. You need each generated test to arrive as a record with a stable id, status, and failure message so the gate can be driven automatically. What is the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a flagged-test schema block to CLAUDE.md with worked examples, so Claude learns to emit each generated test with a stable id, status, and failure message from project context.
options.explanation: Putting format examples in CLAUDE.md is prompt-based guidance that the model follows only probabilistically. At a 1-in-8 failure cost that flips the gate, you cannot accept occasional missing or malformed fields; project-context examples do not guarantee every record is present and parseable on every run.

options.label: B
options.text: Sharpen the generation prompt with few-shot examples of high-signal tests so fewer flaky or duplicate cases slip through before the gate evaluates them.
options.explanation: Better few-shot examples improve the quality and relevance of the generated tests, but that is a different problem. The gate is failing because the output is not reliably structured, not because the tests are low-value; raising test quality does nothing to make the records parseable.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the CLI with `--output-format json` and pass `--json-schema` defining the required fields, then convert the guaranteed JSON to JUnit XML for the gate.
options.explanation: Correct. Enforcing the schema at the CLI level guarantees well-formed JSON with the id, status, and failure-message fields on every run, so the conversion to JUnit XML is a deterministic transform rather than a flaky parse. This removes the probabilistic extraction step that was flipping the gate.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the prose test report and add a regex post-processor that scans the narrative for test names and pass or fail keywords to assemble the JUnit XML the gate ingests.
options.explanation: This just rebuilds the brittle extractor that already mis-reads 1 in 8 runs. Regex over free-form narrative remains fragile to phrasing changes and adds a maintenance burden, when enforcing structured output at the source eliminates the parsing risk entirely.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the Claude Code CLI's `--output-format json` with `--json-schema` enforces structured output at the tool level, guaranteeing the required fields every run so the JUnit conversion is deterministic. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap (CLAUDE.md examples are followed only probabilistically). B solves an adjacent problem (test quality, not parseable structure). D re-creates the same brittle narrative-parsing failure the scenario is trying to eliminate.

questions.text: Your CI bot uses Claude to triage performance concerns on pull requests, instructed to "flag changes that introduce significant slowdowns." Across last sprint's 90 merged PRs, the same kind of change got labeled a blocker on one PR and waved through on another, and reviewers stopped trusting the severity field. What change addresses the root cause of this inconsistent triage?

options.label: A
options.text: Lower the sampling temperature to near zero so the triage call becomes effectively deterministic and returns the same severity each time it runs
options.explanation: Driving temperature toward zero does make a given call reproducible, which is tempting because the symptom looks like flakiness. But the variance here is across different PRs measured against an undefined bar for 'significant', not the same diff scored twice. A deterministic call with no criterion just applies its implicit, shifting bar more reliably, so the cross-PR inconsistency remains.

options.label: B
options.text: Add several few-shot examples of past PRs that were correctly flagged as significant slowdowns so the model can match new diffs to them
options.explanation: Few-shot examples nudge the model toward the shown cases, but it pattern-matches to those exemplars and still lacks the underlying threshold, so novel changes that don't resemble the examples get judged inconsistently—the same gap that caused the problem.

options.label: C
options.text: Have Claude rank each PR's changes against the rest of that PR and flag the single worst offender in each PR as significant
options.explanation: Ranking severity relative to each PR's own changes makes the label depend on its neighbors, so an identical change is a blocker in a clean PR and acceptable in a noisy one—this relative framing reintroduces the very inconsistency being fixed.

options.label: D
options.text: Define 'significant' as fixed, absolute thresholds, such as a documented added-latency budget, that Claude applies to every PR
options.explanation: Replacing the vague word with concrete, absolute criteria gives Claude one fixed bar to apply to every PR, so the same kind of change is judged the same way regardless of which PR it lands in—directly removing the ambiguity that drove the inconsistent severity.

questions.explanation: The root cause is that 'significant' is undefined, so each PR is judged against an implicit, shifting bar. D fixes this by specifying explicit, absolute thresholds Claude applies uniformly. A (near-zero temperature) only makes a single call reproducible; it targets run-to-run variance on the same input, not the missing definition across different PRs, so the cross-PR inconsistency survives. B (few-shot examples) makes the model pattern-match to exemplars without giving it the underlying threshold, so novel diffs stay inconsistent. C (rank relative to the rest of the PR) ties severity to each PR's neighbors, which reintroduces the inconsistency rather than removing it.

questions.text: Your CI/CD dashboard flags one Claude job as the pipeline's largest line item: a `release-notes-draft` step that summarizes every merged PR. Nobody waits on it interactively, but it has a hard deadline — its output must be posted before the 08:00 release-readiness review, roughly two hours after the nightly run kicks off. You want to halve the bill with the Message Batches API (50% discount, asynchronous, results retrieved by polling, may take up to ~24h with no completion-time guarantee). What is the best move?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep `release-notes-draft` on synchronous calls — the Batches API cannot meet its two-hour deadline.
options.explanation: Correct. The discount is the trade for accepting up-to-24h, no-guarantee turnaround, and this job has a hard two-hour budget — so the Batches API simply does not fit, and the 50% savings is not available to this workload without risking a missed review. Being non-blocking is necessary but not sufficient; you also need a latency budget that clears the worst case.

options.label: B
options.text: Submit it as a batch — the job is non-blocking and runs in the background, so it is a textbook batch workload.
options.explanation: The recall trap. "Background, non-blocking" is the usual signal to batch, but it ignores the hard two-hour deadline. The Batches API gives no completion-time guarantee (up to ~24h), so a batch could easily blow past the 08:00 review — non-blocking does not make a deadline-bound job batchable.

options.label: C
options.text: Call the Batches API in "priority" mode so it keeps the 50% discount but returns inside the two-hour window.
options.explanation: Invented mechanism. There is no synchronous or priority tier of the Message Batches API — the 50% discount exists precisely because you accept asynchronous, poll-based delivery with no timing guarantee. You cannot buy back the deadline and keep the discount.

options.label: D
options.text: Build an in-house batching service with a nightly worker that queues the calls, retries them off-peak, and pages on-call if the 08:00 deadline is at risk.
options.explanation: Over-engineering. A custom queue, retry layer, and deadline pager is a large amount of new infrastructure to chase a discount that off-peak scheduling on the standard API does not even earn — standard calls are full price regardless of when they run. The deadline rules out batch; the right move is the minimal one, not a new service.

questions.explanation: A is correct: the workload is non-blocking but carries a hard two-hour deadline, and the Batches API's up-to-24h, no-guarantee turnaround cannot satisfy it — so the 50% discount is off the table here and the job stays synchronous. B is the recall trap: "background/non-blocking" usually signals batch, but it overlooks the deadline; with no completion-time guarantee a batch can miss the 08:00 review. C invents a synchronous/"priority" batch tier that does not exist — the discount is the trade for async, poll-based delivery. D over-engineers a custom queue/worker/pager that earns no discount on standard calls and is far more than the situation needs.

questions.text: Your Claude-powered PR reviewer organizes its findings into named rule packs and posts each as an inline comment. A weekly report tags every comment authors mark "wrong call," and the rates split sharply by pack: the auth-vuln pack sits at 7%, the race-condition pack at 13%, and the resource-leak pack at 10%, but the import-ordering pack lands at 49% and the comment-style pack at 55%. Last Tuesday a maintainer opened a proposal to demote the reviewer to advisory-only, and three teams have already added an editor rule that auto-collapses every comment the bot posts on sight — so its accurate concurrency and security catches are vanishing along with the noise. Which approach best rebuilds confidence in the reviewer while still moving it forward?

options.label: A
options.text: Train a meta-model on the historical "wrong call" labels so the reviewer can score each candidate finding and auto-suppress the ones it predicts authors will reject, regardless of which pack produced them.
options.explanation: Wrong (over-engineering plus a brittle proxy). A learned suppressor optimizes against past reject labels rather than fixing the two packs that are actually noisy, and it can silently start dropping correct security or concurrency findings whenever they resemble previously-rejected ones — the opposite of rebuilding trust.

options.label: B
options.text: Stop the two low-precision packs (import-ordering and comment-style) from posting at all, let only the trusted packs comment, and revise those two packs separately before switching them back on.
options.explanation: Correct. The wrong-call rate is concentrated in two identifiable packs, so muting exactly those restores signal immediately — the trusted packs keep catching real issues and stop being collateral in the auto-collapse — while the noisy packs are fixed offline and reintroduced only once their precision is acceptable.

options.label: C
options.text: Set up a standing human triage rotation where an on-call engineer reviews and approves every finding from the two noisy packs before it reaches the PR, keeping that gate in place permanently.
options.explanation: Wrong (manual band-aid that does not scale). A permanent human gate spends engineer time on every noisy finding indefinitely and never improves the packs themselves; it manages the symptom rather than fixing the low-precision rules.

options.label: D
options.text: Raise a single global confidence threshold applied uniformly to all five packs until the blended wrong-call rate across the whole reviewer falls under the team's target.
options.explanation: Wrong (a uniform knob for a pack-specific problem). One global threshold ignores that the noise lives in two packs: pushing it high enough to silence them also suppresses findings from the trusted packs — losing the very security and concurrency catches you need — while the blended average hides which packs are still misfiring.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the wrong-call rate is concentrated in two packs, so muting just those restores trust at once (the good packs keep posting and escape the auto-collapse) and the noisy packs are fixed offline before returning. A trains a global suppressor that can quietly drop correct findings. C is a permanent human gate that does not scale or improve the packs. D applies one uniform threshold to a pack-specific problem, suppressing the trusted packs along with the noisy ones.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline asks Claude to generate unit tests for each changed file. The tests are functionally correct, but a quarterly audit finds the suite is hard to maintain: roughly 30% of generated tests skip arrange-act-assert structure, test names range from "test1" to long sentences, and some bundle five assertions into one case while others split trivially. You rewrote the prompt to say "follow arrange-act-assert, use descriptive snake_case names, and one logical assertion per test," but the next run is still a mix—some files come out clean, others ignore the rules entirely. Which prompting change most reliably makes the generated tests structurally consistent?

options.label: A
options.text: Tighten the instructions further by spelling out an explicit rule for each convention—naming pattern, section ordering, and assertion count—so the model has an unambiguous specification to follow
options.explanation: Wrong: the scenario already shows that adding explicit convention rules produced an inconsistent mix. Restating the same abstract instructions in more detail repeats the approach that just failed, because models follow described rules less reliably than demonstrated patterns.

options.label: B
options.text: Increase the context Claude receives by including the full file under test plus its imported modules, so it has enough information to write well-formed tests
options.explanation: Wrong: this misdiagnoses the problem. Some files already come out cleanly structured with the current context, so missing information is not the cause—the variability is in formatting, not in test correctness, and more context does not address format consistency.

options.label: C
options.text: Add 3-4 few-shot examples in the prompt showing complete tests in the exact target style: snake_case name, arrange-act-assert sections, one assertion each
options.explanation: Correct: few-shot examples give the model a concrete pattern to imitate, which yields far more consistent output formatting than abstract instructions when those instructions have already proven unreliable. Showing complete tests in the desired style demonstrates every convention at once.

options.label: D
options.text: Split the work into a generation pass that writes the tests and a separate reformatting pass that rewrites each one to match the conventions
options.explanation: Wrong: a two-pass pipeline adds architectural complexity to compensate for an unreliable first prompt instead of fixing it. The reformatting pass inherits the same instruction-following weakness, so consistency is not guaranteed and the simpler fix is to demonstrate the format up front.

questions.explanation: The concept is that when instruction refinement fails to produce consistent output format, few-shot examples are the most reliable lever—so C is correct. A repeats the failing approach (more explicit instructions, which the stem already shows didn't work). B misdiagnoses it as an information/context gap when clean output already appears with the current context, so the issue is formatting, not missing data. D over-engineers with a two-pass pipeline whose reformatting step inherits the same instruction-following unreliability. C wins because demonstrated examples set a concrete pattern the model imitates consistently.

questions.topic: Cutting Per-Comment Triage Time in Automated Review
questions.text: Your CI pipeline posts an average of 22 review comments per pull request through the `claude-review` GitHub Action. A team retrospective times the developer workflow and isolates one dominant cost: for each comment, the developer opens the changed file, scrolls to the line the comment points at, and reads the surrounding code to reconstruct which rule was triggered before accepting or dismissing it — roughly two minutes of that lookup work per comment. The team wants to drive down that per-comment lookup time. Which change best targets it?

options.label: A
options.text: Stand up a separate classifier service that learns from past accept/dismiss decisions and prunes comments it predicts the team will dismiss, before the Action posts them
options.explanation: Wrong on two counts. It is premature complexity — a whole learned service — when the retrospective pointed at a per-comment lookup cost, not the comment count. And even if it worked, pruning the list leaves every surviving comment exactly as expensive to triage as before; it shrinks volume rather than cutting the open-file-and-locate-the-line work that each remaining comment still demands.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a rotating human triager who pre-reads the posted comments each morning and tags the ones the rest of the team should prioritize looking at first
options.explanation: Wrong: this is a manual band-aid that does not scale and does not touch the measured cost. The triager still has to do the same open-file-find-line lookup per comment, and so does everyone after them; it adds a person and a step without making any single comment faster to judge.

options.label: C
options.text: Have Claude re-sort the posted comments so that blocking security issues float to the top of the PR thread and stylistic notes sink to the bottom of it
options.explanation: Wrong: re-ordering solves an adjacent problem — which comment to read first — but leaves the per-comment cost untouched. The developer still opens the file, finds the line, and reconstructs the rule for every comment regardless of where it sits in the thread, and that lookup is what the retrospective measured.

options.label: D
options.text: Have each comment quote the exact offending lines and name the specific rule it triggered, inline
options.explanation: Correct: embedding the quoted lines and the named rule inside each comment lets the developer judge it in place, removing the open-the-file-and-locate-the-line lookup that drives the two-minute cost. This attacks the per-comment work the retrospective isolated, which none of the other options do.

questions.explanation: D is the single defensible answer: putting the offending lines and the triggered rule inline inside each comment eliminates the open-file, find-line, reconstruct-the-rule lookup that the retrospective measured as the two-minute per-comment cost — it makes each individual comment faster to judge. A is over-engineering plus off-target: a learned classifier reduces the number of comments, not the lookup cost of the ones that remain. B is a manual band-aid that adds a person and a step but leaves the same per-comment lookup in place and does not scale. C fixes an adjacent problem (reading order), not the per-comment cost, since every comment still requires the same file-and-line investigation wherever it appears in the thread. Only D reduces the cost the team actually wants to cut.

questions.topic: Workflow vs agent for a routine refactor
questions.text: Your platform team ships a project subagent that performs one mechanical refactor across 200 engineers' repos: rename a deprecated helper to its replacement and update every import. The steps are identical on every run. Telemetry shows the subagent's autonomous loop varies run-to-run on the same input — 6 to 19 tool calls, token cost swinging 3x, and the produced diff occasionally missing or over-touching files. What is the most effective way to make this refactor reliable and cheap?

options.label: A
options.text: Replace the autonomous subagent with a fixed workflow that runs the rename and import-update as a predefined sequence of steps.
options.explanation: Correct. The task is well-defined with identical, predictable steps, so Anthropic's guidance points to a workflow (a coded, fixed path) rather than an agent. Removing the model-driven loop eliminates the run-to-run variance, caps tool calls, and stabilizes cost — the dynamic control the agent provides buys nothing here.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the subagent but add stronger instructions and few-shot refactor examples to its CLAUDE.md so it behaves consistently.
options.explanation: Wrong (taxonomy #1, prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee). Better prompting only shifts the probability distribution of an autonomous loop; it cannot make a fundamentally non-deterministic agent produce the same bounded steps every run. The fix for needed determinism is structural, not a prompt tweak.

options.label: C
options.text: Introduce an orchestrator agent with a routing classifier that inspects each repo, then dispatches the refactor to specialized worker subagents per language and framework so the loop converges.
options.explanation: Wrong (taxonomy #3, over-engineering). A single mechanical rename does not need a router, a classifier, or a multi-agent orchestrator-worker topology. Adding agents multiplies the very nondeterminism and cost you are trying to remove; the minimal effective change is the opposite direction.

options.label: D
options.text: Give the subagent more tools and expose additional internal docs through the MCP server so it has the context to finish the refactor correctly.
options.explanation: Wrong (taxonomy #2, solves an adjacent problem). More tools and MCP context address capability gaps, but the subagent already completes the task — its problem is unbounded, variable execution on a fixed procedure. Extra context does not constrain the loop or control cost.

questions.explanation: A is correct: a refactor whose steps are identical and predictable on every run is the textbook case for a workflow (a fixed, coded sequence) rather than an agent, per Anthropic's "Building effective agents" — the autonomous loop's dynamic control adds only variance, latency, and token cost here. B fails because prompt/CLAUDE.md tuning cannot make a probabilistic loop deterministic. C fails as over-engineering — a router plus multi-agent topology adds nondeterminism and cost to a one-step task. D fails by fixing an adjacent issue (capability/context) when the actual problem is unbounded execution of a known procedure.

questions.topic: Subagent context isolation for repo-wide analysis
questions.text: A staff engineer runs a custom `/audit-deprecation` slash command that asks Claude Code to find every caller of a deprecated `legacyAuth()` helper across the ~3,200-file monorepo and summarize the migration risk. Telemetry shows the main agent reads files into its own context until the run aborts with a context-window-exceeded error after touching only about 40% of the directories, and the partial report misses several call sites. What is the most effective way to architect this repo-wide analysis?

options.label: A
options.text: Raise the run's `max_tokens` and instruct the agent to load the full repository into context up front so it can reason over every file in a single pass.
options.explanation: Wrong: `max_tokens` caps the model's output, not the input context window, and even a larger window cannot hold a 3,200-file monorepo. This is the context-stuffing symptom fix that caused the abort, not a fix for it.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the coordinator dispatch parallel subagents, each scoped to a slice of the tree, that search their slice and return only condensed findings to the main agent.
options.explanation: Correct: subagent context isolation is Anthropic's documented orchestrator-worker pattern for wide exploration. Each subagent burns its own context window searching its slice and returns a compact summary, so the coordinator's context stays small and the whole repo gets covered.

options.label: C
options.text: Stand up a separate vector-database indexing microservice that pre-embeds the monorepo nightly, and have the agent query it over MCP instead of reading files.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is premature complexity. A new indexing service is heavy infrastructure for a task the documented subagent fan-out already solves, and stale nightly embeddings can miss the exact call sites a code audit must find.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a rule to `CLAUDE.md` telling the agent to stay concise, skip irrelevant files, and keep only the most relevant code in context as it works.
options.explanation: Wrong: a prompt instruction cannot guarantee a single agent stays within a fixed context budget across thousands of files. It relies on probabilistic compliance where a deterministic architectural split is needed.

questions.explanation: B is correct: scoping the work into parallel subagents that each search a slice of the tree and return only condensed findings is Anthropic's orchestrator-worker pattern for wide exploration — it keeps the coordinator's context small while still covering all 3,200 files. A misreads `max_tokens` (it caps output, not the input window) and still tries to stuff the whole repo into one context. C adds a heavyweight nightly indexing service whose stale embeddings can miss the exact call sites an audit must find. D relies on a prompt instruction to stay within budget, which cannot guarantee coverage across thousands of files.

questions.text: Your developer-productivity rollout includes a `/refactor` subagent that rewrites code across multiple files in one run. Telemetry from a two-week pilot shows it applied edits that overwrote or deleted files in 9% of runs before any engineer saw the change, and three of those landed broken code on a branch. You want to keep the agent autonomous for analysis but make the irreversible apply step safe. What is the most effective way to add human-in-the-loop control?

options.label: A
options.text: Add explicit instructions to the shared CLAUDE.md telling the refactor subagent to always pause and ask the engineer for confirmation before it overwrites or deletes any file.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. A CLAUDE.md instruction shapes behavior probabilistically, so the agent can still skip the pause on some fraction of runs. For an irreversible write you need a deterministic gate the model cannot talk itself past, not a stronger instruction.

options.label: B
options.text: Build a separate risk-classification service that scores each proposed refactor and automatically routes high-risk ones to a different model while low-risk ones apply directly.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is premature complexity, and it still auto-applies changes with no person in the loop. It answers a routing question that was not asked and adds a service instead of the simple, documented fix of gating the apply step on human approval.

options.label: C
options.text: Require the agent's destructive apply step to pass through a permission checkpoint that presents the planned diff and blocks execution until a human approves it.
options.explanation: Correct: a code-level permission/approval gate is a deterministic guardrail. The agent can plan and propose autonomously, but the irreversible write only runs after explicit human approval of the diff, which is the documented human-in-the-loop pattern for high-stakes actions.

options.label: D
options.text: Lower the model temperature on the refactor subagent and ask engineers to review every resulting pull request more carefully before merging it.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is a manual band-aid that acts after the fact. Lowering temperature does not stop the irreversible overwrite, and post-hoc PR review does not protect the files the agent already destroyed during the run, so it leaves the actual risk unguarded.

questions.explanation: C is correct: gating the irreversible apply step on a permission checkpoint that shows the planned diff and blocks until a human approves is a deterministic human-in-the-loop guardrail — the agent still plans autonomously, but the destructive write cannot run without approval. A relies on a CLAUDE.md instruction the agent can skip on some runs. B adds a routing service that still auto-applies with no person in the loop. D lowers temperature and asks for more careful after-the-fact review, neither of which stops an unreviewed destructive write.

questions.text: To roll out a shared lint config, your platform team runs an orchestrator agent that dispatches one worker subagent per repository to edit the config and open a PR across five repos. A run's logs show the orchestrator's final message read "all 5 repositories updated," yet only 4 PRs exist; in the missing repo a worker hit a merge conflict, and that worker's result never reached the orchestrator. What is the most effective architectural change to prevent the orchestrator from reporting success on incomplete coordinated work?

options.label: A
options.text: Have each worker blind-retry its repository up to three times on any error before the orchestrator composes its final summary of the run.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A merge conflict will not resolve on a blind retry, and retrying still leaves the orchestrator with no signal that the repo ultimately failed, so it can still claim success. Retry policy is orthogonal to making the failure visible to the coordinator.

options.label: B
options.text: Strengthen the worker prompt and CLAUDE.md so that every worker always reports its final status, including any failures, back to the orchestrator.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This relies on probabilistic LLM compliance for a correctness guarantee; the cost of even occasional silent drops is a false success on a multi-repo change. The propagation of a worker's outcome should be enforced in the orchestration code, not requested via instructions.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a separate reconciliation service that, after each run, diffs all five repositories against the target config and flags any that were not updated.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is an after-the-fact verification layer that over-engineers the fix; it detects drift later but does not stop the orchestrator from declaring success in the same run, and it duplicates state the orchestration could track directly.

options.label: D
options.text: Have each worker return a structured success-or-failure result that the orchestrator must aggregate before summarizing, treating any missing or failed result as a non-success.
options.explanation: Correct. Surfacing each worker's outcome to the coordinator and gating the summary on every result being present and successful is Anthropic's documented orchestrator-worker pattern. It makes the conflict visible so the orchestrator can re-plan or escalate instead of reporting a false success.

questions.explanation: The root issue is that a worker's failure never propagated, letting the orchestrator summarize success on incomplete coordinated work. D fixes this at the architectural level: workers return a structured success/failure result, and the orchestrator aggregates and gates its summary on every result being present and successful — Anthropic's guidance to make failures visible to the controlling agent. A (blind retry) does not resolve a merge conflict and still leaves the outcome invisible. B (stronger prompts/CLAUDE.md) substitutes probabilistic LLM compliance for a deterministic guarantee. C (a separate reconciliation service) is after-the-fact over-engineering that detects drift later but does not prevent the false success in the run.

questions.topic: Expose internal docs via MCP vs pasting into the prompt
questions.text: Your rollout dashboard shows the shared CLAUDE.md has grown to ~28K tokens after teams pasted the full internal engineering handbook into it, and platform metrics report it is re-sent on every Claude Code session, inflating per-session input cost. A review of edits finds the handbook changes several times a week, yet any given /review or /test run touches only one or two of its ~40 sections. You already operate an MCP server that fronts this documentation. What is the most effective way to give the agents this internal documentation?

options.label: A
options.text: Move the handbook behind the existing MCP server as a retrievable resource and drop it from CLAUDE.md, so each run pulls only the section it needs.
options.explanation: Correct. Just-in-time retrieval through the MCP server you already run loads only the relevant section per task, always serves the current version (no staleness), and removes the 28K-token blob from every session. It addresses all three observations -- bloat, freshness, and cost -- with the minimal change of reusing existing infrastructure.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the full handbook pasted into the shared CLAUDE.md so every agent always holds the complete documentation directly in context.
options.explanation: Wrong (wrong location / context bloat). This is the status quo the metrics indict: it re-sends 28K tokens every session, goes stale within days because the handbook changes several times a week, and forces ~40 sections of irrelevant content into context when only one or two are used.

options.label: C
options.text: Stand up a separate vector database with an embeddings pipeline, index the handbook into it, and run a new retrieval service that the agents query for each task.
options.explanation: Wrong (over-engineering). Building a parallel RAG service duplicates capability you already have in the MCP server. The exam rewards the minimal effective change; a new service adds operational cost and a re-indexing burden for the frequent edits without beating on-demand MCP retrieval.

options.label: D
options.text: Leave the handbook in CLAUDE.md and enable prompt caching on that block so the repeated documentation costs less to re-send each session.
options.explanation: Wrong (solves an adjacent problem). Caching only attacks the cost symptom; it still loads all ~40 sections regardless of relevance and cache entries are invalidated whenever the frequently-edited handbook changes, so it fixes neither the bloat nor the staleness the dashboard flagged.

questions.explanation: A is correct: routing the docs through the MCP server already in place gives just-in-time retrieval of only the needed section, always-current content, and removes the 28K-token blob from every session -- satisfying all three observations (bloat, freshness, cost) with the least new machinery. B is the indicted status quo (re-sends everything, goes stale, loads irrelevant sections). C over-engineers by building a vector DB / RAG service that duplicates the existing MCP server and adds re-indexing overhead for frequent edits. D treats only the cost symptom via caching while still loading all sections and breaking the cache on every frequent edit.

questions.topic: Tool description quality for an internal API tool
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes two similar internal-docs tools to the agents: search_runbooks (for on-call/incident procedures) and search_design_docs (for architecture RFCs). Traces show the model calls search_runbooks for architecture questions in 22% of invocations and frequently passes an empty query string, so engineers get irrelevant or empty results. What is the most effective first step to reduce this misuse?

options.label: A
options.text: Add routing guidance to the shared CLAUDE.md explaining when to prefer search_runbooks over search_design_docs, so the model learns the distinction from project context.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-when-the-fix-is-tool-local trap. CLAUDE.md is a weaker, non-local lever than the tool's own model-facing description, and it does nothing about the empty-query argument, which lives in the parameter documentation. Anthropic's guidance is to invest in the tool and parameter descriptions themselves.
