options.label: B
options.text: Rewrite each tool's description and parameter docs to state what it covers, when to choose it over the sibling tool, and that query must be non-empty.
options.explanation: Correct. The description and parameter documentation are the highest-leverage, model-facing surface for a tool. Treating it like onboarding a new engineer - what the tool covers, when to pick it over the sibling, and what each parameter requires - directly resolves both the wrong-tool selection (22%) and the empty-query argument. It is the minimal documented fix the exam rewards.

options.label: C
options.text: Build a classifier or routing layer in front of the MCP server that inspects each incoming question and decides which of the two search tools the agent should invoke.
options.explanation: This is the over-engineering trap. A new routing service is premature complexity when a documented, minimal fix - clearer tool and parameter descriptions - addresses the same misuse. It also adds latency and a maintenance burden while still leaving the empty-query argument unfixed.

options.label: D
options.text: Add retry-with-backoff and richer error handling so empty or irrelevant results are automatically retried against both tools until one returns content.
options.explanation: This solves an adjacent problem. Retries and error handling improve resilience to failures but do not teach the model which tool to pick or that query must be non-empty; they would re-issue the same malformed calls and mask the root cause rather than reduce misuse.

questions.explanation: The failure is tool-local: the model picks the wrong one of two similar internal tools and passes an empty required argument. The single defensible fix is B - rewrite each tool's description and parameter docs, since that documentation is the primary model-facing surface governing both tool selection and argument quality. A pushes guidance into CLAUDE.md, a weaker non-local lever that never touches the empty-query parameter (prompt-when-the-fix-is-tool-local). C builds a classifier/routing layer - premature complexity over the minimal documented change, and still leaves the empty query unfixed. D adds retries/error handling, which addresses an adjacent resilience concern but never teaches correct tool choice or argument shape. Only B resolves the observed misuse at its source.

questions.topic: MCP transport: stdio vs Streamable HTTP for a shared server
questions.text: Your internal-docs MCP server currently ships with each engineer's Claude Code config as a stdio server. A platform telemetry report across the 200 engineers shows every client spawns its own local copy of the server as a subprocess, each maintaining a separate documentation index, and there is no shared endpoint: a docs update only reaches an engineer after they pull the latest index and restart their client, so indexes drift for days. You want one authoritative server the whole org connects to. Which change best fixes this?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep the stdio server local to each engineer, but add a scheduled git-pull-and-restart hook so every machine refreshes its index nightly.
options.explanation: This is a distribution band-aid (taxonomy #2/#10): it automates refreshing 200 separate local indexes but preserves 200 subprocesses, no shared endpoint, and stale-until-restart state. It addresses how updates propagate, not the root problem that the transport is single-client by design.

options.label: B
options.text: Migrate the server to the HTTP+SSE transport with `--transport sse`, the recommended transport for remote MCP servers, so clients connect over the network.
options.explanation: Wrong because the premise is false: HTTP+SSE was superseded by Streamable HTTP in the MCP spec (2025-03-26 revision), and `--transport sse` is retained only for backward compatibility with old clients. Picking the deprecated transport when the current one exists is choosing a superseded mechanism dressed up as the documented one (taxonomy #5).

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy the server once as a centralized remote service on the Streamable HTTP transport, and point every client at it with `claude mcp add --transport http`.
options.explanation: Correct. Streamable HTTP is the current transport for a standalone server that many clients reach over the network: one deployment, one shared index, central updates and auth. Switching off stdio removes the per-client subprocess and the drift in one move.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the stdio server running locally on each machine, but stand up a shared sync-and-proxy service that brokers a common index and forwards each local subprocess's requests so all 200 instances stay consistent.
options.explanation: Over-engineering (taxonomy #3): it builds a bespoke service to reinvent exactly what the Streamable HTTP transport already provides natively — one remote endpoint with a shared index that many clients connect to. It keeps the 200 subprocesses you are trying to eliminate and adds a proxy layer that the documented transport makes unnecessary.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the stdio transport runs the server as a per-client subprocess, which is precisely why 200 engineers get 200 separate, drifting indexes with no shared endpoint. Streamable HTTP is the current MCP transport for a single standalone server that many clients connect to remotely, giving one authoritative index with central updates and auth (`claude mcp add --transport http`). A only automates refreshing the 200 local copies, leaving the single-client transport in place. B selects HTTP+SSE, which was superseded by Streamable HTTP in the 2025-03-26 spec and survives only for backward compatibility, so it is the wrong (deprecated) transport. D keeps stdio and builds a custom sync/proxy layer to reinvent the shared remote endpoint that Streamable HTTP already provides, retaining the very subprocesses that cause the drift.

questions.topic: CLAUDE.md hierarchy for shared team conventions
questions.text: During the Claude Code rollout to ~200 engineers, an onboarding survey reports that newcomers' sessions keep ignoring the team's house rules (use pnpm not npm, conventional-commit messages, no edits under generated/). The conventions are correct and stable; you want every engineer to pick them up automatically when they clone the repo, with no per-developer setup. What is the most effective way to share these conventions?

options.label: A
options.text: Have each engineer copy the conventions into their own ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md on their machine.
options.explanation: Wrong scope (taxonomy #7). The user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is machine-local and unversioned, so it only affects the one developer who edits it and requires manual per-developer setup, exactly the opposite of auto-loading on clone.

options.label: B
options.text: Pin the conventions in the team Slack channel and ask engineers to paste the relevant ones into their prompt at the start of each session.
options.explanation: Manual, non-scaling band-aid (taxonomy #10). It depends on every engineer remembering to copy the rules into each session; nothing is loaded automatically and compliance degrades exactly as the survey shows.

options.label: C
options.text: Commit a README.md documenting the conventions to the repository root so it ships with every clone.
options.explanation: Solves an adjacent problem (taxonomy #2). A committed README.md is version-controlled and present on clone, but Claude Code does not auto-load README.md into session context, so the agent sessions keep ignoring the rules, exactly the symptom the survey flags.

options.label: D
options.text: Commit a CLAUDE.md containing the conventions to the repository root and have the team pull it.
options.explanation: Correct. The project-level CLAUDE.md is the documented file Claude Code reads automatically into context at session start, so committing it gives all 200 engineers the same shared conventions on clone with no per-developer setup.

questions.explanation: D is correct: a project root CLAUDE.md is the documented, version-controlled file Claude Code reads automatically into context at session start, so committing it shares stable team conventions with every engineer who clones the repo, requiring no per-developer action. A is the right idea in the wrong scope, the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is machine-local and unshared. B is a manual band-aid that does not scale and reintroduces the inconsistency the survey flagged. C commits a real, version-controlled file, but Claude Code does not auto-load README.md the way it loads CLAUDE.md, so the sessions keep ignoring the rules.

questions.topic: Custom slash command for a repeated workflow
questions.text: Your rollout dashboard shows engineers running the same prompt by hand dozens of times a day: "open a draft pull request for the changes on the current branch, write a summary, and link the Jira ticket." Usage logs show the wording drifts between people, so the PR descriptions are inconsistent. You want to ship this as a versioned `/open-pr` slash command in `.claude/commands/` where the developer passes the ticket key at invocation time, e.g. `/open-pr PROJ-1423`. What is the most effective way to author the command so it uses that ticket key?

options.label: A
options.text: Write the prompt in `.claude/commands/open-pr.md` and reference the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder where the ticket key should go.
options.explanation: Correct. A custom slash command is a Markdown file whose body is the prompt; Claude Code substitutes whatever the developer types after the command name into the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder. This is the documented first-party mechanism for a parameterized, version-controlled command, giving every engineer one consistent prompt that still accepts a per-invocation ticket key.

options.label: B
options.text: Define the command in `.claude/config.json` with a `commands` array and an `args` schema that captures the ticket key.
options.explanation: Wrong. There is no `.claude/config.json` `commands` array or `args` schema in Claude Code; commands are individual Markdown files in `.claude/commands/`. This is a plausible-sounding but invented configuration surface, so it would never load the command.

options.label: C
options.text: Build a project subagent that watches the branch and an MCP tool that creates the PR, then have engineers invoke the subagent instead of a slash command.
options.explanation: Wrong. This over-engineers a problem the documented slash-command mechanism already solves. The need is a single repeatable, parameterized prompt, not a new subagent plus MCP tool; adding that infrastructure is more to build and maintain without addressing the consistency goal any better than a command file.

options.label: D
options.text: Hardcode a representative ticket key in `open-pr.md` and tell developers in `CLAUDE.md` to edit the file before each run.
options.explanation: Wrong. Hardcoding a value and asking each engineer to hand-edit the command before every run is a manual band-aid that does not scale and reintroduces the very drift and inconsistency the command is meant to eliminate; it also ignores the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder built for exactly this case.

questions.explanation: A is correct: Claude Code custom slash commands are Markdown files in `.claude/commands/`, and the `$ARGUMENTS` placeholder injects whatever the developer types after the command name, so `/open-pr PROJ-1423` passes the ticket key into one shared, version-controlled prompt. B invents a `.claude/config.json` `commands`/`args` mechanism that does not exist. C over-engineers the task with a subagent plus an MCP tool when a single parameterized command file is the minimal effective fix. D hardcodes the key and relies on manual per-run edits, a band-aid that does not scale and recreates the inconsistency being fixed.

questions.topic: Plan mode vs direct execution for a risky migration
questions.text: A postmortem from last sprint shows that an engineer asked Claude Code to migrate a service from the old logging library to the new one in a single direct pass; Claude edited 43 files before stopping, and 9 of those edits used the wrong import path and had to be hand-reverted, costing half a day. Another sweeping framework migration is scheduled across the same repo. What is the most effective way to run the next migration so the team can catch bad edits before they land?

options.label: A
options.text: Launch the migration with `claude --dry-run-migrate` so Claude previews every file change and writes nothing until the flag is removed.
options.explanation: Invented-but-plausible flag. There is no `--dry-run-migrate` flag in Claude Code; the real read-only review surface is Plan mode (Shift+Tab or `--permission-mode plan`). A made-up flag does nothing, so this gives no safety at all.

options.label: B
options.text: Run the migration in Plan mode so Claude analyzes the repo read-only and proposes a plan, which the engineer approves before any files are edited.
options.explanation: Correct. Plan mode runs Claude with read-only tools and surfaces a reviewable plan; no edits are written until the engineer approves, which is exactly the before-it-lands checkpoint the postmortem calls for.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a CLAUDE.md rule telling Claude to always show its plan first and to be extra careful with import paths during any migration.
options.explanation: Prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee. A CLAUDE.md instruction is a probabilistic nudge Claude may not follow under a 43-file sweep; it is not a hard gate that blocks edits until a human approves, so the wrong edits can still land.

options.label: D
options.text: Build a project subagent backed by the docs MCP server that validates each proposed migration edit against the new API before Claude applies it.
options.explanation: Over-engineering. Standing up a custom validator subagent and MCP wiring is heavy work that duplicates a built-in capability; Plan mode already gives a read-only, human-approved review step as the minimal effective fix.

questions.explanation: B is correct: Claude Code's Plan mode (entered with Shift+Tab or `--permission-mode plan`) runs read-only, produces a plan, and writes no file edits until the human approves — precisely the catch-bad-edits-before-they-land checkpoint the postmortem demands for a risky migration. A is an invented flag (`--dry-run-migrate` does not exist) so it provides no real preview. C relies on a CLAUDE.md instruction, a probabilistic nudge rather than a deterministic gate, so bad edits can still be written. D over-engineers a custom validator subagent plus MCP wiring when the built-in Plan mode already provides the human-approved, read-only review step as the minimal effective change.

questions.text: Telemetry on your shared `/review` slash command shows engineers act on only 28% of its comments. Reviewing the transcripts, the same defect class is tagged "critical" in one run and "minor" in another, some comments name a file and line while others are vague paragraphs, and many lack any concrete fix. The command prompt currently says only "Review this diff and report any problems you find." Across 200 engineers, what is the most effective change to make the output consistent and actionable?

options.label: A
options.text: Instruct the command to rank each finding's severity relative to the worst issue in the current diff, so the single most serious problem in every PR is always the one flagged as critical.
options.explanation: Ranking severity relative to the current diff is exactly the relative/proportional trap: a trivial PR's worst issue gets labeled "critical" while a serious bug in a clean PR gets downgraded, so the same defect class still lands in different tiers across runs. Normalizing within each batch reintroduces the inconsistency you are trying to remove instead of anchoring to fixed criteria.

options.label: B
options.text: Append "be thorough and explain your reasoning in detail for every issue" to the command prompt to raise the depth and quality of each comment.
options.explanation: Telling the model to be more thorough addresses an adjacent problem (comment depth/verbosity), not the consistency or actionability gap. Longer reasoning does not pin severity labels to stable definitions or force a file:line plus a suggested fix, so the variance and the missing remediation steps persist.

options.label: C
options.text: Rewrite the prompt to require a fixed per-finding schema: file:line, a severity from defined tiers anchored to concrete examples, and a suggested fix.
options.explanation: Specifying an explicit output structure with severity tiers defined by concrete examples is the documented prompt-engineering fix for both problems at once: the fixed schema forces every comment to carry file:line and a concrete remediation (actionability), and the example-anchored tiers give the model absolute, stable criteria so identical defects get the same label across runs (consistency).

options.label: D
options.text: Set up a rotating duty where two senior engineers manually triage and relabel the command's output each day before the rest of the team reads it.
options.explanation: A human triage rota is a manual band-aid that does not change the system producing the inconsistent output; it cannot scale to 200 engineers, adds review latency, and leaves the prompt itself just as likely to mislabel severity and omit fixes the next day.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the inconsistency and low action rate stem from an underspecified prompt, and the documented fix is to constrain the output—a fixed per-finding schema (file:line, suggested fix) makes every comment actionable, while severity tiers anchored to concrete examples give absolute criteria so the same defect is labeled the same way every run. A applies relative/proportional scoring, which normalizes within each PR and recreates the cross-run variance. B fixes an adjacent problem (depth/verbosity) but never pins severity definitions or mandates file:line and a fix. D is a manual human band-aid that does not change the prompt and cannot scale across 200 engineers. Only C addresses the root cause with the minimal effective change.

questions.topic: Reducing hallucinated/nonexistent APIs in generated code
questions.text: Engineers report that code from your `/review` and `/test` commands fails at runtime because it calls internal-SDK methods that do not exist — plausible names like `client.getCustomerV2()` and `orders.batchFetch()` that the real library never defined. This affects roughly 9% of generated files, and the invented names track the SDK's naming style rather than any method the model was actually shown; the model is writing from parametric memory instead of the authoritative interface (whose signatures the team's MCP documentation server already exposes). What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a firm rule to the shared CLAUDE.md instructing the model to never call an internal SDK method unless it is certain the method actually exists.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is the prompt-when-you-need-grounding trap. A stronger instruction asks the model to police its own parametric memory, which is exactly what produced the invented names. The model cannot reliably know what it does not know, so the hallucination rate persists; the gap is missing ground truth in context, not missing emphasis.

options.label: B
options.text: Lower the sampling temperature for the code-generation calls and add a few-shot block of idiomatic, correctly-formatted SDK usage examples so the model has concrete patterns to imitate for each call it writes.
options.explanation: Wrong: this solves an adjacent problem. Lower temperature only steadies style and consistency, and a few-shot block demonstrates a handful of methods — it does not enumerate the full interface or tell the model whether the specific method this call needs actually exists. The model can confidently and consistently emit a nonexistent method, so the knowledge gap is untouched.

options.label: C
options.text: Enable Claude Code's built-in --verify-apis flag so the harness automatically rejects any generated call to an SDK method that is not real.
options.explanation: Wrong: invented-but-plausible flag. Claude Code has no --verify-apis option; this fabricates a guardrail that sounds real. Choosing it signals unfamiliarity with the actual surface and does nothing, since the flag does not exist.

options.label: D
options.text: Pull the relevant SDK signatures from the existing MCP documentation server into context before generation, so the model writes against the real interface.
options.explanation: Correct: hallucinated APIs come from generating off parametric memory. Retrieving the authoritative signatures the MCP server already exposes and placing them in context gives the model the real interface to copy from rather than guess, directly cutting invented method calls. It supplies ground truth instead of relying on the model's self-restraint, and reuses infrastructure the team already has.

questions.explanation: The defect is hallucinated API calls: generated code invokes plausible-but-nonexistent methods on the internal SDK because the model draws on parametric memory instead of the authoritative reference. The fix that addresses the actual cause is D — retrieve the real SDK signatures from the MCP documentation server (which the team already runs) into context before generation, so the model writes against the true interface; this is grounding, not self-restraint. A is the prompt-when-you-need-grounding trap: a stronger CLAUDE.md rule asks the model to police memory it cannot audit, so hallucinations persist. B solves an adjacent problem: lower temperature and a few idiomatic examples fix style and consistency but never enumerate which methods exist. C is a fabricated flag — Claude Code has no --verify-apis option. Only D supplies the ground truth that closes the knowledge gap.

questions.topic: Structured output for a tool consuming Claude's result
questions.text: One of your internal tools calls the Messages API to summarize a pull request and then parses Claude's reply as JSON to populate fields in your dashboard. Production logs show the JSON parse step throwing on about 7% of calls because Claude sometimes prefixes a sentence of prose or wraps the object in a ```json fence. What is the most reliable way to get output the tool can consume?

options.label: A
options.text: Define the target shape as a tool input schema and call the API with tool_choice forcing that tool, then read the structured tool_use input.
options.explanation: Correct. Defining the fields as a tool's input schema and forcing the tool with tool_choice makes Claude return its result as structured tool_use input that conforms to the schema, which the program reads directly. This removes the fragile text-parsing step entirely instead of hoping the model formats prose correctly.

options.label: B
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt to say 'Respond with only valid JSON and no other text,' and add an example of the exact object.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A stronger instruction nudges behavior but is still probabilistic, so a fraction of responses will keep including prose or a code fence. When a downstream tool must parse every response, you need the deterministic guarantee of a schema, not better wording that the model may still ignore.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep prompting for JSON but add a regex that extracts the first {...} substring from the reply before parsing.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Scraping the first brace-delimited substring is a brittle heuristic that breaks on nested objects, truncated output, or multiple blocks, and it papers over the real issue rather than using the API's structured-output mechanism that returns clean schema-conformant data.

options.label: D
options.text: Send Claude's reply to a second Messages API call that reformats it into clean JSON before the tool parses it.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A second reformatting call adds latency, token cost, and another probabilistic step that can itself emit stray prose. It is premature complexity when a single forced-tool call already returns parseable structured input on the first request.

questions.explanation: A is the single defensible answer: defining the output as a tool input schema and forcing it with tool_choice makes Claude return schema-conformant tool_use input the program reads directly, eliminating text parsing — Anthropic's documented mechanism for output a program must consume. B is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: better instructions stay probabilistic and will keep failing on some calls. C is a brittle heuristic that substitutes regex scraping for the real structured-output mechanism and breaks on nested or truncated output. D is over-engineering: a second reformatting call adds cost, latency, and another fallible LLM step when one forced-tool call already returns parseable data.

questions.topic: Context budgeting for a huge codebase
questions.text: Telemetry from your 200-engineer rollout shows that on the largest monorepo, sessions routinely paste whole directory dumps into the chat; those sessions hit the context limit roughly 30% of the time and average 4x the token cost of other teams, with no measurable quality gain. You want a sustainable way to give Claude Code enough context on this huge codebase without blowing the context budget. What is the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Build a retrieval-augmented pipeline: embed every file in the repository into a vector store and, on each request, inject the top-k most similar chunks into the prompt so Claude sees the relevant code.
options.explanation: This is premature complexity. Claude Code already retrieves files agentically via Glob/Grep/Read; bolting a separate embeddings index in front of it duplicates that capability, adds infrastructure to maintain, and injects fixed top-k chunks that are often the wrong granularity. It is the over-engineered answer where a concise CLAUDE.md plus on-demand search is the documented, minimal fix.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep a concise CLAUDE.md with project conventions and an architecture map, and let Claude Code use its agentic file search to read only the files relevant to each task on demand.
options.explanation: Correct. Anthropic's guidance for large codebases is exactly this: a small, curated CLAUDE.md (conventions, key commands, a map of where things live) gives durable orientation, and Claude Code pulls in only the files it needs per task through agentic search. Context stays small and relevant, which directly fixes the limit hits and token cost.

options.label: C
options.text: Paste the full directory tree and the contents of the core modules into CLAUDE.md so every session starts with the whole codebase already loaded in context.
options.explanation: This recreates the exact problem the telemetry shows. CLAUDE.md is loaded into context every session, so stuffing the tree and core modules into it makes every request expensive and pushes toward the context limit even when the task touches only a few files. The point is to budget context, not to preload everything.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch all engineers to a model with the 1M-token context window so the entire codebase fits in a single request and no file ever has to be omitted.
options.explanation: This solves an adjacent problem. A larger window raises the ceiling but does not make dumping the whole repo a good idea: it still costs tokens, dilutes attention with irrelevant code, and degrades as the window fills. Fitting everything in is not the goal; including only what each task needs is.

questions.explanation: The defensible answer is B: for a huge codebase, give Claude Code durable orientation through a concise CLAUDE.md (conventions plus an architecture map) and rely on its agentic, on-demand file retrieval to load only task-relevant files — keeping context small and directly fixing the limit hits and 4x token cost. A is over-engineering: a separate embeddings/RAG index duplicates Claude Code's built-in search and adds maintenance for fixed-granularity chunks. C reproduces the observed failure by preloading the tree and core modules into the always-loaded CLAUDE.md. D solves an adjacent problem — a bigger window raises the ceiling but still pays for, and is distracted by, irrelevant code; the goal is relevance, not fitting everything in.

questions.topic: Prompt caching for repeated large context
questions.text: One of your internal tools calls the Messages API directly to answer engineer questions, prepending the same ~30K-token internal-docs preamble (with a `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint on it) to every request. The preamble's first line is a system-prompt header that reads "Internal docs assistant — generated at {timestamp}". Across hundreds of back-to-back requests, the response `usage` shows `cache_creation_input_tokens` around 30K on every call and `cache_read_input_tokens` of 0 every time, and per-request input cost never drops. What is the most likely root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: The breakpoint is below the model's minimum cacheable prefix, so the 30K-token preamble silently fails to cache on every request.
options.explanation: Wrong: 30K tokens is far above every model's minimum (4096 on Opus, 2048 on Sonnet/Fable). A prefix too short to cache reports `cache_creation_input_tokens: 0`, not ~30K — the metrics show the cache is being written each time, which rules this out.

options.label: B
options.text: Ephemeral entries expire after 5 minutes; raising the TTL to one hour with `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral", "ttl": "1h"}` is required for back-to-back requests to hit the cache.
options.explanation: Wrong: the requests are back-to-back, well inside the 5-minute TTL, so expiry is not the cause — and a longer TTL still keys on identical prefix bytes, so it would not produce a single hit here. This is an adjacent caching knob, not the root cause.

options.label: C
options.text: The `{timestamp}` interpolated into the system-prompt header changes the prefix bytes every request, so the cache writes a fresh entry each call and never finds a prior one to read.
options.explanation: Correct: prompt caching is a prefix match keyed on exact bytes up to the breakpoint. A per-request timestamp at the front of `system` makes every prefix unique — the documented silent-invalidator signature of non-zero `cache_creation` with zero `cache_read`. Move the timestamp after the breakpoint (or remove it) and the cache reads.

options.label: D
options.text: Direct Messages API calls need `cache_mode: "shared"` set on the request to reuse a cache entry written by a previous call rather than treating each call as isolated.
options.explanation: Wrong: there is no `cache_mode` parameter — caching is automatic for any request whose prefix bytes match a live entry. Cross-request reuse is the default behavior, not an opt-in flag, so this fabricated parameter is not the fix.

questions.explanation: C is correct: interpolating a live `{timestamp}` into the cached prefix changes the prefix bytes on every request, so each call writes a fresh cache entry and never matches a prior one — exactly the `cache_creation` on every call with `cache_read` of zero that the usage shows. A is wrong because a sub-minimum prefix would not report ~30K `cache_creation_input_tokens` each call. B misdiagnoses TTL: back-to-back requests sit well within the 5-minute window, and the misses are byte mismatches, not expiry. D invents a `cache_mode: "shared"` parameter that does not exist.

questions.topic: Workflow vs agent for a fixed extraction pipeline
questions.text: A design-review report on your invoice-extraction pipeline notes that all documents already pass through the same fixed stages (parse, call Claude with the emit_invoice tool, validate against the schema, write the record) at thousands of documents per day, with steady cost-per-document. The report proposes "agentifying" it so the model can dynamically decide its own steps when fields are missing, ambiguous, or low-confidence. Following Anthropic's guidance on agents versus workflows, what is the most appropriate architecture for this pipeline?

options.label: A
options.text: Build an orchestrator-worker multi-agent system with a separate subagent per target field (vendor, amount, currency, due date, line items) coordinating to assemble the emit_invoice record.
options.explanation: Wrong: over-engineering (premature complexity). Splitting one structured extraction into a coordinator plus a subagent per field multiplies token cost, latency, and failure surface across thousands of documents per day, for steps that are already fixed and reliably handled by a single tool call. Multi-agent designs are warranted for parallelizable open-ended research, not a deterministic field-extraction sequence.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the workflow for clean documents but route any record with missing, ambiguous, or low-confidence fields into an autonomous agent loop that decides its own steps and tool calls until it judges the record complete.
options.explanation: Wrong: grants autonomy a bounded path does not need. This is genuinely an architecture choice, but the problem cases are still known and finite, so handing them to a model-directed loop reintroduces nondeterministic iteration counts, variable latency and cost, and harder auditing exactly where a fintech accounting pipeline most needs predictability. Anthropic reserves agent control flow for steps that cannot be specified in advance, not for a bounded escalation path that deterministic code and human review handle better.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep it as a deterministic workflow; handle ambiguity in code by enforcing the schema and routing low-confidence records to human review, reserving agent autonomy for open-ended tasks.
options.explanation: Correct. The steps are fixed and known ahead of time, so Anthropic's guidance favors a predefined workflow over an autonomous agent. Missing or low-confidence values are handled with explicit, deterministic code (schema validation plus confidence-threshold routing to human review), preserving predictability, cost, and auditability. Agent autonomy is reserved for problems whose steps cannot be specified in advance.

options.label: D
options.text: Convert it to a single autonomous agent that loops, choosing which tools to call and when to stop, continuing to re-examine the document until it judges every field sufficiently confident before emitting the record.
options.explanation: Wrong: over-engineering by granting autonomy a fixed pipeline does not need. Letting the model direct its own control flow introduces nondeterministic iteration counts, variable latency and cost, and harder auditing, with no benefit when the stages are already known. Anthropic recommends agents only when steps cannot be predetermined.

questions.explanation: The pipeline's steps are fixed and known in advance, which is the defining condition under Anthropic's guidance for choosing a workflow (predefined code paths) over an agent (model directs its own control flow), so C is correct: keep the deterministic workflow and handle ambiguity with explicit code (schema enforcement plus routing low-confidence records to human review). A over-engineers with a multi-agent orchestrator-worker design unjustified for a fixed sequence. B is the closest distractor but still grants model-directed autonomy to a bounded, known set of cases that deterministic code plus human escalation handle with better predictability, cost, and auditability. D grants full agent autonomy and nondeterministic looping that a predetermined pipeline does not need.

questions.text: Your invoice extraction pipeline ingests three known source streams — digital invoices, remittance emails, and scanned PDFs — and calls Claude with the emit_invoice tool through the Messages API. The weekly accuracy report breaks field-level accuracy down by stream: 96% on digital invoices, 88% on remittance emails, but only 71% on scanned PDFs, where the single shared prompt frequently misreads totals and currency. What is the most effective change to close the accuracy gap?

options.label: A
options.text: Strengthen the single shared system prompt with detailed instructions and a few well-chosen extraction examples so the model handles scanned documents more carefully.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. Adding instructions and few-shot examples to one prompt cannot give scanned PDFs the different (vision-oriented) handling they need; it relies on probabilistic compliance and leaves the per-type gap unaddressed.

options.label: B
options.text: Tighten the emit_invoice input schema and add stricter post-validation so malformed or low-confidence records are rejected before they reach accounting.
options.explanation: This solves an adjacent problem. Stricter schema and validation police the output shape and catch bad records, but they do not improve extraction accuracy on scans — rejecting wrong values is not the same as reading them correctly, so the gap remains.

options.label: C
options.text: Build and train a dedicated document-classification service that inspects each incoming file and predicts which of the three categories it belongs to before extraction.
options.explanation: This is over-engineering. The document type is already known from the source stream, so a separate classifier adds a service and training cost to rediscover information you already have, without touching the extraction quality on scans.

options.label: D
options.text: Route each document by its already-known type to a stream-specific extraction path, giving scanned PDFs handling tuned for image input.
options.explanation: Correct. This is the routing-workflow pattern: a single prompt optimized for clean text underperforms on scans, so splitting into type-specific paths and giving scans vision-tuned handling directly closes the type-correlated gap, using the type the pipeline already tracks.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the report shows a type-correlated failure (71% on scanned PDFs vs 96% on digital invoices), which is the textbook signal for a routing workflow — optimizing one shared prompt for all inputs hurts the input that differs most. Routing each already-known stream to a specialized path (vision-tuned handling for scans) addresses the actual cause. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: more instructions and few-shot cannot substitute for the different handling scans require. B solves an adjacent problem — schema and validation enforce output shape, not extraction accuracy. C over-engineers by building a classifier to determine a type the pipeline already knows from the source stream, without improving accuracy on scans.

questions.topic: Escalating low-confidence extractions to a human
questions.text: The weekly accuracy report shows that about 9% of invoices auto-posted by your extraction pipeline were later corrected by accounting, and the errors cluster in scanned PDFs with faint text where Claude fills in a plausible amount or due date that turns out to be wrong. You want to catch these uncertain extractions before the JSON record reaches the accounting system. What is the most effective way to handle them?

options.label: A
options.text: Extend the emit_invoice schema so Claude can mark a field as low-confidence (or null with a reason), and have your code route any flagged record to a human review queue before posting.
options.explanation: Correct. This gives the model a documented way to signal uncertainty instead of fabricating a value, and it enforces the escalation deterministically in code rather than relying on the model to remember to escalate. Both halves are what make it reliable.

options.label: B
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt to instruct Claude to always extract a value for every field and never leave one blank.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: it removes the model's ability to abstain, which forces it to guess on exactly the faint-text cases that are already failing, making hallucination more likely rather than less.

options.label: C
options.text: Train a separate classifier on past corrections to predict which extracted records are probably wrong, then post the rest automatically.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is premature complexity. The model can already self-report its confidence through the schema, so building and maintaining a separate predictor is redundant work that the minimal documented fix avoids.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a manual checkpoint where an operations analyst opens and verifies every one of the thousands of extracted records each day against the source document before it is posted to accounting.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is a manual band-aid that does not scale to thousands of documents per day and discards the automation entirely, instead of selectively escalating only the uncertain extractions.

questions.explanation: A is correct because it combines the two documented practices for low-confidence extraction: let the model signal uncertainty (extend the emit_invoice schema to flag low-confidence or null-with-reason fields so it abstains instead of fabricating) and enforce the escalation in deterministic code that routes flagged records to a human queue. B is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee distractor — forbidding blanks forces guessing on the very faint-text cases that fail. C over-engineers with a separate classifier when the model can self-report confidence. D is a manual band-aid that reviews everything and does not scale, rather than escalating only uncertain cases.

questions.text: A weekly accuracy report on your invoice extraction pipeline shows that 100% of emitted records pass emit_invoice schema validation, yet manual audit finds that ~9% of records drawn from ambiguous documents (multi-currency invoices, scanned remittance emails) carry a confidently wrong vendor or amount while remaining perfectly well-formed JSON. You want to raise field-level accuracy on exactly these hard cases without adding human review for the thousands of documents processed per day. Which change is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Tighten the emit_invoice input schema with stricter types, regex-constrained currency codes, and required fields so malformed extractions are rejected before they reach accounting.
options.explanation: Adjacent-problem distractor. The report already states every record passes schema validation; the errors are well-formed but semantically wrong. Stricter schema constraints catch structural defects, not a confidently mis-read vendor or amount, so they leave the measured 9% untouched.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a separate evaluator call that scores each record against the source document on explicit criteria, then re-extracts the failing fields until they pass.
options.explanation: Correct. This is the evaluator-optimizer pattern from Anthropic's guidance: a separate evaluator grades output against clear criteria and feeds correction back to the generator in a loop. With clear criteria and value from iteration, it directly targets well-formed-but-wrong fields on the ambiguous cases without human review.

options.label: C
options.text: Add several few-shot examples of tricky multi-currency and scanned-remittance documents to the extraction prompt and strengthen the instructions to read amounts and vendors carefully.
options.explanation: Prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee distractor. Few-shot examples and firmer wording raise the odds of a correct first pass but add no self-correction; the model can still emit a confident, well-formed wrong value on a novel ambiguous document, which is exactly the failure being measured.

options.label: D
options.text: Build a document-type classifier that routes invoices, remittance emails, and scanned PDFs each to its own specialized single-pass extraction prompt before the emit_invoice tool call is ever made.
options.explanation: Over-engineering distractor. A routing layer is a heavier build that improves prompt specialization, but specialized prompts are still single-pass extractions with no verification, so the same well-formed-but-wrong outputs on ambiguous documents survive. It does not check the output against the source.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the failure is well-formed records that are semantically wrong on ambiguous documents, which calls for the evaluator-optimizer loop — a separate evaluator call scores each record against the source on explicit criteria, then the generator re-extracts the failing fields until they pass, all without human review. A is an adjacent-problem trap: schema tightening fixes malformed output, but the report says 100% of records already pass validation. C is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: few-shot and stronger instructions improve first-pass odds probabilistically but add no self-correction. D is over-engineering: a classifier plus specialized prompts is a heavier build that still does single-pass extraction with no verification against the source.

questions.topic: Reliable JSON via tool use vs prompt-and-parse
questions.text: In the invoice-extraction pipeline, production logs show that 9% of documents fail with JSONDecodeError: the team currently prompts Claude to "respond only with a JSON object" and then runs json.loads() on the raw text, which sometimes wraps the object in prose or a markdown fence. Which change most reliably produces parseable records matching the target shape?

options.label: A
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt with explicit instructions and three few-shot examples that always end with a clean JSON object, reminding Claude never to add prose or code fences.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. Better instructions and examples only raise the probability of valid JSON; they cannot eliminate the 9% parse failures because output still depends on probabilistic compliance, and the downstream accounting system needs a deterministic guarantee.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the prompt-and-parse flow but add a regex that strips markdown fences and grabs the first {...} span, retrying json.loads() up to three times on failure.
options.explanation: This is the brittle-heuristic trap: scraping JSON out of free text with a regex and blind retries patches symptoms rather than using the first-party mechanism. It still breaks on nested braces, truncation, or prose, and retries waste batch budget without changing the underlying output contract.

options.label: C
options.text: Call the model with the emit_invoice tool and read the structured input from its tool_use block instead of parsing prose.
options.explanation: Correct. Defining emit_invoice with an input_schema and reading the tool_use block's input is Anthropic's documented mechanism for structured output: the model returns a structured object conforming to the JSON Schema, so you no longer parse free text and the parse-failure class disappears.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a response_format: "json" parameter to the Messages API request so the endpoint forces every completion to be returned as a valid JSON document.
options.explanation: This is the invented-but-plausible-API trap. The Messages API has no response_format JSON-mode parameter; structured output is achieved through tool use with an input_schema, so this fix targets a flag that does not exist.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the documented way to get reliable structured JSON from the Messages API is tool use — define the emit_invoice tool with an input_schema and read the tool_use block's input, which conforms to the schema and removes the prose-parsing step entirely, eliminating the JSONDecodeError class. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap (instructions and few-shot only raise the odds, they don't guarantee parseable output for a deterministic downstream consumer). B is the brittle-heuristic trap (regex extraction plus blind retries patches symptoms instead of using the first-party mechanism). D is the invented-but-plausible-API trap (the Messages API has no response_format JSON mode; structured output comes from tools with input_schema).

questions.topic: Forcing tool use to guarantee the schema
questions.text: In your invoice-extraction pipeline, you call the Messages API with a single tool, emit_invoice, whose input_schema defines the target JSON record. Batch logs show that for roughly 9% of documents Claude replies with a free-text summary and no tool_use block at all, so those records silently never reach the accounting system. Which change most reliably guarantees that every response conforms to the emit_invoice schema?

options.label: A
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt to insist that Claude must always call emit_invoice, and add several few-shot examples showing documents paired with the exact JSON output you expect.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap. Better instructions and few-shot examples raise compliance but remain probabilistic, so the 9% of free-text responses can still occur; it cannot guarantee a schema-conformant output.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave tool_choice on its default and add a regex parser over the assistant text that validates the fields and re-sends the request whenever it fails to find a valid record.
options.explanation: This is a brittle post-hoc heuristic that reacts after the model has already skipped the tool. Default tool_choice still lets Claude answer in prose, and parsing or retrying text does not guarantee a tool_use block conforming to input_schema.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a response_format parameter to the Messages API request set to a JSON-schema object that mirrors the emit_invoice input_schema, so the API rejects non-conforming output.
options.explanation: This invents a parameter from another provider's API. Anthropic's Messages API has no response_format field; structured output is obtained through tools, so this change does nothing to force schema conformance.

options.label: D
options.text: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "emit_invoice"} on the request so Claude is forced to call that tool.
options.explanation: Correct. Forcing the specific tool makes Claude return a tool_use block whose arguments are validated against emit_invoice's input_schema on every call, deterministically eliminating the free-text, no-tool responses.

questions.explanation: D is correct: setting tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "emit_invoice"} forces Claude to invoke that specific tool on every request, so each response is a tool_use block whose arguments conform to the tool's input_schema, deterministically removing the ~9% free-text misses. A is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap: stronger instructions and few-shot examples improve but never guarantee compliance, leaving the failure tail. B is a brittle heuristic (regex over text plus blind retry) that reacts after the model has already skipped the tool rather than forcing structured output. C invents response_format, an OpenAI-style parameter that does not exist on Anthropic's Messages API, where structured output comes from tools.

questions.topic: Handling schema validation failures / tool errors
questions.text: Batch telemetry shows that for 9% of documents, Claude's `emit_invoice` call returns arguments that fail your post-call JSON Schema validation — usually `amount` sent as a formatted string ("1,250.00") where the schema requires a number, or `due_date` in a non-ISO format. Your pipeline currently logs the failure and writes a null record downstream. You want these documents corrected without a separate manual queue and without weakening the strict schema. What is the most effective change?

options.label: A
options.text: On a validation failure, return a `tool_result` with `is_error: true` naming the specific field and constraint that failed, and let Claude retry the `emit_invoice` call on the next turn.
options.explanation: Correct. Anthropic's tool-use guidance is to surface tool/validation errors back to the model as a tool_result with is_error set and concrete detail, so Claude can self-correct on a follow-up turn. The deterministic schema stays the authoritative gate (it is never weakened), and the specific field/constraint message gives Claude exactly what it needs to reformat amount or due_date. This is the minimal effective, documented mechanism and keeps the strict schema intact.

options.label: B
options.text: Strengthen the system prompt with explicit instructions and few-shot examples requiring `amount` as a bare number and `due_date` in ISO 8601, so the model formats every field correctly.
options.explanation: Incorrect — prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee. Better instructions and examples may lower the failure rate but rely on probabilistic compliance; at thousands of documents per day a residual few percent of malformed outputs still produces null records. It does not provide a deterministic correction path for the failures that slip through, which is what the scenario requires.

options.label: C
options.text: Drop documents whose `emit_invoice` arguments fail validation into a discard log and continue the batch, since the strict schema is doing its job by rejecting bad records.
options.explanation: Incorrect — silent swallow. Discarding (or nulling) the record hides a recoverable failure rather than feeding it back so the model can fix it. The asks were to correct these documents without a manual queue; silently dropping 9% of records loses data and defeats the goal.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a deterministic post-processor that strips commas from `amount` and reparses common date formats into ISO, coercing the failed arguments into a schema-valid record before downstream.
options.explanation: Incorrect — over-engineering with a brittle heuristic. A bespoke coercion layer only handles the formats you anticipate, silently mangles edge cases (e.g. European decimal commas, ambiguous dates), and grows endlessly as new malformations appear. The documented fix is to return the error to the model and let it self-correct, not to rebuild extraction logic in fragile post-processing code.

questions.explanation: A is correct: returning a `tool_result` with `is_error: true` that names the failed field and constraint lets Claude see exactly what was wrong and retry the `emit_invoice` call with corrected arguments — the documented tool-error feedback loop, which keeps the strict schema intact and needs no manual queue. B relies on prompting to prevent all malformations, which cannot guarantee schema-valid output. C simply discards the failing 9% rather than correcting them. D hard-codes coercion for only the two failure modes seen today and reshapes data outside the model, which is brittle and breaks on the next unseen format.

questions.topic: CLAUDE.md for the pipeline repo conventions
questions.text: A code-review report on the invoice-extraction repo shows that across the last 20 PRs, Claude Code keeps re-deriving the same repo conventions from scratch each session: it puts the currency-normalization helper in the wrong module, forgets that low-confidence fields must be emitted as null in the emit_invoice payload, and re-discovers where the JSON schema lives. Every PR wastes turns relearning rules that never change. What is the most effective way to stop this recurring waste?

options.label: A
options.text: Add the conventions to the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md so they load in every session on the developer's machine.
options.explanation: Wrong scope: a user-level CLAUDE.md is personal and machine-specific, so it does not travel with the repo and is not shared with teammates or CI. Project conventions belong in the repo so every contributor and session inherits them.

options.label: B
options.text: Record the stable conventions (helper location, the null-on-low-confidence rule, the schema path) in the project-root CLAUDE.md.
options.explanation: Correct: CLAUDE.md at the project root is auto-loaded into context for every Claude Code session in that repo and is committed alongside the code, so durable, repo-specific conventions are known up front instead of rediscovered each time.

options.label: C
options.text: Create a /conventions slash command in .claude/commands/ and instruct everyone to run it before starting any extraction task.
options.explanation: Solves an adjacent problem and adds friction: a slash command supplies on-demand context only when someone remembers to invoke it, whereas the issue is that the conventions should be present automatically every session. CLAUDE.md is the always-loaded surface.

options.label: D
options.text: Paste the full emit_invoice input schema and a long convention list into the first user message of each new session as a preamble.
options.explanation: Manual band-aid that does not scale: it depends on each developer remembering to paste it, bloats every prompt, and drifts from the source of truth. The repo-committed CLAUDE.md captures stable conventions once and loads them for all.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the project-root CLAUDE.md is automatically loaded into context at the start of every Claude Code session in the repo and is version-controlled with the code, which is exactly the right home for durable, repo-specific conventions (helper module location, the null-on-low-confidence emission rule, the schema path) so they are not relearned each session. A is wrong scope (taxonomy #7): the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is personal and does not ship with the repo or reach teammates and CI. C solves an adjacent problem (taxonomy #2/#3): a slash command provides context only when manually invoked, not automatically every session. D is a manual, non-scaling band-aid (taxonomy #10) that relies on humans pasting context each time and drifts from the committed source of truth.

questions.topic: Slash command to scaffold a new extractor
questions.text: A retro report on the invoice-extraction pipeline notes that engineers hand-copied the same boilerplate for a new `emit_invoice`-style extractor 14 times last quarter, with inconsistent results. The lead wants every engineer who clones the repo to scaffold a new extractor identically by running `/new-extractor remittance-email` inside Claude Code. What is the correct way to add this shared, version-controlled slash command?

options.label: A
options.text: Create the command at `~/.claude/commands/new-extractor.md` so it is available whenever you run Claude Code on the project.
options.explanation: Wrong scope (trap #7). A user-level command at `~/.claude/commands/` works for the engineer who created it but lives outside the repo, so cloning the project does not give the rest of the team the command. The stem explicitly requires it be shared and version-controlled.

options.label: B
options.text: Register the command by adding a `commands` array entry that maps `new-extractor` to the scaffold prompt in `.claude/settings.json`.
options.explanation: Invented surface (trap #6). Claude Code does not define custom slash commands through a `commands` key in `.claude/settings.json`; that file holds permissions, hooks, and env config. The real mechanism is a Markdown file under `.claude/commands/`, so this entry has no effect.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a `.claude/commands/new-extractor.md` file containing the scaffold prompt and commit it to the repo.
options.explanation: Correct. A Markdown file in the project's `.claude/commands/` directory becomes a slash command named after the file (`new-extractor.md` -> `/new-extractor`), and `$ARGUMENTS` injects the passed name. Because it lives in the repo, every engineer who clones it gets `/new-extractor` automatically.

options.label: D
options.text: Stand up an MCP server that exposes a `scaffold_extractor` tool, run it locally, and have each engineer configure their client to connect to it before generating a new extractor file from the tool's output.
options.explanation: Over-engineering (trap #3). A whole MCP server plus per-engineer client configuration is far heavier than the documented one-file solution and reintroduces the inconsistent per-engineer setup the team is trying to eliminate. A committed slash command is the minimal effective change.

questions.explanation: C is correct: a project-scoped custom slash command is just a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/`, where the filename becomes the command name and `$ARGUMENTS` carries the argument; committing it makes it shared and version-controlled for everyone who clones the repo, exactly as the stem requires. A is the right idea in the wrong scope -- `~/.claude/commands/` is user-level and is not shared via the repo. B invents a `commands` array in `.claude/settings.json` that does not exist; settings.json does not register slash commands. D over-engineers the task with an MCP server and per-engineer setup when a single committed file suffices.

questions.topic: CI test of extraction accuracy on a fixture set
questions.text: Your nightly batch report shows the `due_date` field emitted by the `emit_invoice` tool was correct on only 88% of documents, and accounting contractually requires at least 95% field-level accuracy before records flow downstream. You want a CI test in the Claude Code repo that gates merges on extraction accuracy, and you have a versioned fixture set of documents each paired with a hand-labeled, known-correct JSON record. How should the CI test be structured?

options.label: A
options.text: Capture Claude's full text response for each fixture once, store it as a golden snapshot, and fail CI when a new run's response text does not match the stored string byte-for-byte.
options.explanation: Brittle-heuristic trap: the contract is the structured `emit_invoice` tool input, not the prose, which is nondeterministic. Snapshotting raw response text will flag spurious diffs on every run while never actually measuring field-level extraction accuracy.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a second Claude call in CI that acts as an LLM judge, sending it each extracted record and the source document and asking it to score whether the extraction looks correct, then fail on a low average judge score.
options.explanation: Over-engineering trap: an LLM judge is the tool for when you lack ground truth. Here every fixture already has a hand-labeled correct record, so a deterministic field comparison is strictly better, cheaper, and exactly reproducible; the judge adds cost and its own error.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the pipeline on the fixtures and compare this run's overall accuracy to the previous run's, failing CI only when accuracy drops below the prior number (a regression gate).
options.explanation: Relative-instead-of-absolute trap: regression gating lets accuracy ratchet downward over many small drops while never breaching the prior step, so it can sit far under the contractual 95% floor without ever failing. The requirement is a fixed absolute threshold, not no-regression.

options.label: D
options.text: Run the pipeline on the fixtures, compare each emitted `emit_invoice` field against the labeled record, and fail CI when field-level accuracy falls below the 95% threshold.
options.explanation: Correct: with known-correct labels you compute deterministic per-field accuracy against the structured tool input and gate on the fixed contractual floor. This directly measures the metric the report and the contract care about and is fully reproducible in CI.

questions.explanation: D is correct because the fixtures carry hand-labeled known-correct records, so the CI test should deterministically compare each emitted `emit_invoice` field to its label and fail when field-level accuracy drops below the fixed 95% floor the contract requires. A snapshots nondeterministic prose instead of the structured fields (brittle heuristic). B adds an LLM judge, which is for cases lacking ground truth, not here where labels exist (over-engineering). C gates on regression relative to the previous run, which can drift below the absolute 95% requirement without ever failing (relative instead of absolute).

questions.text: A field-level accuracy report on the invoice extraction pipeline shows the emit_invoice tool is filled out correctly for clean documents, but errors cluster on ambiguous inputs: when a remittance email writes "1,250" with no symbol the model guesses currency inconsistently, and when a due date is phrased "net 30 from receipt" it sometimes invents a calendar date and sometimes leaves the field empty. The schema validates every output and downstream accounting never sees malformed JSON. What is the most effective change to raise accuracy on these ambiguous fields?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a few-shot block to the prompt with 3-4 worked examples that show how to resolve exactly these ambiguous cases (infer currency from the vendor's country, leave due_date null when only relative terms are given).
options.explanation: Correct. The failures are judgment calls on ambiguous content, not malformed output. Few-shot examples are Anthropic's documented lever for teaching the model the desired disambiguation behavior, demonstrating the exact reasoning for implicit currency and relative dates so the model generalizes the policy.

options.label: B
options.text: Tighten the emit_invoice input schema with stricter types and a currency enum so the model is forced into valid values.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This solves an adjacent problem. The schema already guarantees well-formed, valid JSON; the report says malformed output never reaches accounting. Constraining types cannot tell the model which currency or date is actually correct for an ambiguous document, which is the accuracy gap being asked about.

options.label: C
options.text: Build a separate upstream classifier model that predicts currency and resolves dates, then pass its outputs into the extraction call.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is premature complexity. Adding and maintaining a second model is heavier than the documented minimal fix, and a classifier with no document context is unlikely to beat in-context few-shot guidance on the same ambiguity.

options.label: D
options.text: Route any document containing an implicit currency or a relative due date to a human reviewer before emitting the record.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This is a manual band-aid that does not improve the model's extraction at all and does not scale to thousands of documents per day. It avoids the accuracy problem rather than fixing it.

questions.explanation: A is correct: the failures are reasoning ambiguities (which currency to infer, how to treat a relative due date), and a few-shot block that demonstrates exactly how to resolve those cases is the documented prompt-engineering fix for inconsistent judgment. B tightens the schema, but the schema already rejects malformed JSON — the problem is wrong or empty values that are still type-valid. C builds a separate upstream classifier, premature complexity for what worked examples solve in-prompt. D routes every ambiguous document to a human, which does not scale and abandons the accuracy goal.

questions.topic: Missing fields: null vs omit via schema/prompt
questions.text: Reviewing a day's batch from the extraction pipeline, an accounting engineer reports that when a remittance email carries no due date, the emit_invoice tool call sometimes returns "due_date": null, sometimes omits the due_date key entirely, and occasionally returns "due_date": "unknown". The downstream ingester breaks on the missing key and on the non-date string. What is the most effective way to make Claude emit one consistent representation for a missing due date?

options.label: A
options.text: Append a firm rule to the system prompt — "always include due_date; write null when the document gives no date, never a placeholder string" — plus two few-shot examples showing the null case.
options.explanation: Wrong: this is prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee. Instructions and examples nudge behavior but cannot pin a fixed object shape; across thousands of varied documents some calls still drop the key or invent a string, the exact inconsistency observed.

options.label: B
options.text: In the emit_invoice input schema, keep due_date in required and give it type ["string","null"], stating in its description that an absent date must be emitted as null.
options.explanation: Correct: the tool's input schema is the contract Claude fills. Keeping due_date required removes the omit option, the nullable type sanctions null as the canonical "no date" value, and the description points the model there — so a genuine missing-value path exists and the invented "unknown" sentinel loses its reason to appear.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave the schema unchanged and add a post-processing pass that backfills any missing due_date key and rewrites strings like "unknown" to null before the ingester reads the result.
options.explanation: Wrong: this never changes what Claude emits, which is what the question asks for. It repairs output after the fact and must be extended for every new sentinel ("n/a", "TBD"), patching symptoms instead of fixing the contract the model fills.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a first model pass that classifies whether each document states a due date, then route the two cases to separate extraction prompts so absence is handled in its own path.
options.explanation: Wrong: over-engineering. A classifier and parallel prompts add a service and latency to solve what one schema edit handles; the exam rewards the minimal effective fix, not a premature pipeline split.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the question asks how to make Claude emit one consistent representation, and the missing-value convention belongs in the emit_invoice input schema Claude fills. Keep due_date in required (so the key is never dropped) and give it type ["string","null"] with the null convention in its description; this gives absence a single schema-blessed home as null and removes the gap that makes the model reach for an invented "unknown". A relies on probabilistic prompt compliance where a structural contract is needed. C never changes Claude's emission at all — it repairs output downstream and reopens with each new sentinel string, so it does not answer the ask however robust the cleanup is. D over-engineers with a classifier and a second prompt path when one schema edit suffices. One honest caveat: no input schema is server-side-validated, so the type alone does not hard-block a stray string; B works because it makes null the obvious, sanctioned path and so addresses all three modes at their root, while C addresses none of them at the source.

questions.topic: Prefilling / output-format control for strict JSON
questions.text: In the invoice-extraction pipeline, your parser logs show that about 8% of Messages API responses fail to deserialize because Claude prepends conversational preamble such as "Here is the extracted record:" before the JSON object. You want a reliable way to make Claude's text response begin directly with valid JSON. Which change is the most effective first step?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a firm line to the system prompt instructing Claude to output only the JSON object and never include any introductory text.
options.explanation: This is a prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee distractor. A stronger instruction nudges behavior but still relies on probabilistic compliance, so a portion of responses will keep leaking preamble. It does not deterministically force the first characters of the output.

options.label: B
options.text: Set a response_format parameter to "json" on the Messages API request so the endpoint strips any non-JSON text.
options.explanation: This is an invented-but-plausible API parameter. The Anthropic Messages API has no response_format field that coerces or strips text output, so this configuration would have no effect (or error). It tests whether you know the real API surface.

options.label: C
options.text: Prefill the assistant turn with the opening "{" so Claude continues generating from there and cannot emit a preamble.
options.explanation: Correct. Prefilling the assistant message with the start of the desired output is Anthropic's documented technique for output-format control: Claude continues from the provided characters, so beginning the assistant turn with "{" forces the response to start as a JSON object rather than prose.

options.label: D
options.text: Lower the sampling temperature toward 0 to reduce output variability and discourage the model from adding extra wording.
options.explanation: This solves an adjacent problem. Lower temperature makes outputs more deterministic but does not remove the preamble pattern; if the high-probability completion includes an intro phrase, low temperature can even make that preamble more consistent, not less.

questions.explanation: The reliable, documented lever for output-format control is prefilling the assistant turn, so C is correct: starting the assistant message with "{" forces Claude to continue the JSON object and skip any preamble. A (stronger instruction) is the prompt-when-you-need-a-guarantee trap — it relies on probabilistic compliance and leaves residual failures. B invents a non-existent response_format API parameter. D fixes an adjacent issue (variability) but does not eliminate the introductory text, and can even lock the preamble in.

questions.topic: Long-document position effects (lost in the middle)
questions.text: A nightly accuracy report on the invoice pipeline breaks field-level correctness down by where each value sits in the source document. For short remittance emails all fields score above 97%, but on the long multi-page scanned PDFs a clear pattern appears: vendor and total_amount (which sit in the header and footer) stay at ~96%, while line_items and due_date (which appear in the dense middle pages) drop to 78-81%. The emit_invoice schema, the model, and the few-shot examples are identical across both document types. Which change most directly addresses the accuracy gap on the long PDFs?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a line to the system prompt instructing Claude to pay especially close attention to fields that appear in the middle of long documents before populating the emit_invoice tool.
options.explanation: This is the prompt-as-guarantee trap. Lost-in-the-middle is a structural property of how attention is distributed over a long context, not a motivation problem an instruction can fix. The middle-page fields will still be attended to less reliably, so this offers no deterministic improvement on the 78-81% fields.

options.label: B
options.text: Enrich the emit_invoice input schema with more detailed field descriptions and add three more few-shot examples that show correctly formatted line_items and due_date values.
options.explanation: This solves an adjacent problem. Richer schema descriptions and more examples improve format consistency and reduce malformed output, but the failing fields are not malformed, they are missed or wrong because the model under-attends to the middle pages. Better examples do not change where the information sits in the context window.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a length classifier that detects multi-page PDFs and routes them to a separate higher-tier extraction service with its own retry and reconciliation layer, leaving the short-document path unchanged.
options.explanation: This is premature complexity at the wrong altitude. Routing by length and standing up a second service does not change the within-document attention pattern that causes the gap, it just moves the same long input to another pipeline where the middle pages remain under-attended. It adds operational cost without addressing the root cause.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the model first extract verbatim source quotes for each target field from the document, then populate emit_invoice from those quotes.
options.explanation: Correct. Forcing an explicit quote-extraction pass for each field makes the model do a full, grounded read of the document before committing to values, which is Anthropic's documented mitigation for long-context position effects. It directly lifts the under-attended middle-page fields (line_items, due_date) by anchoring each value to located source text rather than relying on a single pass.

questions.explanation: The report isolates the failure to fields physically located in the middle pages of long PDFs, which is the signature of the lost-in-the-middle position effect: information in the center of a long context is attended to less reliably than information at the start (vendor) or end (total_amount). D is the documented, minimal fix because quote extraction forces a grounded pass that locates each field in source text before emission, directly raising the under-attended middle fields. A is the prompt-as-guarantee trap, an instruction cannot change attention distribution. B solves an adjacent problem, format consistency rather than position-based retrieval. C is over-engineering at the wrong altitude, routing long docs elsewhere leaves the same middle pages under-attended. Only D changes how thoroughly the middle of the document is read.

questions.text: Your invoice-extraction pipeline calls the Messages API with the `emit_invoice` tool, processing about 40,000 documents in a nightly job whose output is consumed by downstream accounting the next morning. Ops reports the nightly run now repeatedly trips the account's requests-per-minute rate limit, and finance flags that monthly token spend has roughly doubled as volume grew. The records are never needed in real time. Which change most effectively cuts cost and relieves the throughput ceiling for this workload?

options.label: A
options.text: Submit the documents through the Message Batches API (`POST /v1/messages/batches`), which processes requests asynchronously at 50% of standard token prices and returns results typically within an hour (up to 24 hours).
options.explanation: Correct. The Batches API is the documented fit for high-volume, non-latency-sensitive work: it bills all token usage at a 50% discount and processes asynchronously off the synchronous rate-limit path, so the nightly 40,000-document job no longer trips the per-minute limit. Because results are only needed by morning, the async window is acceptable, and the discount directly addresses the doubled token spend.

options.label: B
options.text: Add `cache_control` breakpoints to the system prompt and the `emit_invoice` schema so the shared prefix is served from cache on every request.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Prompt caching is compatible with batch processing, but on its own it is the lesser lever here: it discounts only the repeated prefix while each invoice's content and the emitted JSON still bill at full price, and it does nothing about the synchronous requests-per-minute ceiling the nightly run keeps hitting. It is a complement to the Batches API, not the primary fix for cost and throughput at this volume.

options.label: C
options.text: Set `batch: true` on each `messages.create()` call so the Messages API queues the requests at the discounted batch rate.
options.explanation: Incorrect. There is no `batch` flag on the Messages API; batch processing is a separate endpoint (`POST /v1/messages/batches`), not a parameter on a synchronous call. This fabricated flag would error, and even conceptually it would not move the work off the synchronous rate-limit path.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep using synchronous `messages.create()` calls but build an autoscaling worker pool with a token-bucket scheduler that fans out as many concurrent requests as possible to push the documents through faster each night.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Parallelizing synchronous calls reaches the account's rate limit sooner rather than raising it, and every request still bills at full price, so neither the throughput ceiling nor the doubled spend improves. It also reinvents the queuing and discount the Batches API already provides — premature complexity where a documented endpoint is the minimal effective change.

questions.explanation: A is correct: for a high-volume, non-real-time workload, the Message Batches API (`POST /v1/messages/batches`) is the documented way to both cut cost (50% off all token usage) and escape the synchronous requests-per-minute ceiling, with an async completion window (typically under an hour, up to 24 hours) that the morning-consumption deadline easily tolerates. B (prompt caching alone) is a real but adjacent lever — it is compatible with batching and trims the repeated prefix, but leaves per-document content, output tokens, and the synchronous rate limit untouched, so it is a complement, not the fix. C invents a `batch: true` Messages API flag that does not exist (batching is a separate endpoint). D over-engineers a parallel worker pool that hits the rate limit faster, still pays full price, and reinvents what the Batches API already does.
